segments.s650-g73: قباد is the subject (proper name) placed first. 'آن سخن‌گوی را' is the definite object marked by را (object/topic/emphatic marker) meaning 'that spokesman.' پیش here is an adverbial meaning 'before/in the presence of' or 'forward.' خواند is a past tense verb 'called/summoned' (classical/poetic use) taking the preceding object.
segments.s650-g74: ز introduces a cause or source here ('because of' / 'on account of') relating to the following nominal phrase. 'تاراج انبار' is a genitival phrase 'the plundering of the storehouse.' چندی is an adverbial meaning 'for a while.' براند is a third-person past/poetic form of راندن 'to drive' with the prefix بر- ('drove off' / 'drove away'). The clause as a whole says that he drove (him) away for a time because of the plundering.
segments.s650-g75: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the following verb phrase. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave.' پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply' functioning as the direct object of داد. کانوشه (literally 'small chest/casket') follows as a noun phrase in apposition or as the content/character of the answer ('an answer in the form of a casket'), and بدی is adjectival 'evil/bad' qualifying کانوشه.
segments.s650-g76: خرد را: خرد 'wisdom' is marked with را, giving it emphasis or marking it as the affected entity. به گفتار is a prepositional phrase 'in/with respect to speech/words' (به + گفتار). توشه بدی is a noun plus adjective 'a provision/stock that is bad'—the verb ('gave') from the previous segment supplies the action, so this clause says he (or someone) provided wisdom, in words, with a bad provision. Word order is poetic: verb given earlier governs this noun phrase.
segments.s650-g77: سخن 'speech/words' is the head noun; هرچه 'whatever' quantifies it. بشنیدم is the first-person past 'I heard.' از شهریار indicates source 'from the king' (از = 'from/of'). The clause therefore means 'whatever words I heard from the king.'
segments.s650-g78: بگفتم is 'I said / I told' (first-person past); به introduces the indirect object ('to the merchants'). بازاریان is the indirect object 'merchants/shopkeepers.' خوارخوار is an adverbial or evaluative modifier meaning 'contemptible/derisive(ly),' describing the manner in which the speaker relayed the words.
segments.s650-g79: به شاه جهان: به introduces the indirect object 'to the king of the world' (شاه جهان = 'the king of the world', an honorific). گفتم is the speaker's past-tense verb 'I said/told' (here rendered 'I told'). از مار و زهر is a prepositional phrase 'about/of snake(s) and poison' where از marks the topic or content of what was told.
segments.s650-g80: از آن کس که introduces a relative/content clause 'about that person who...' (از = 'about/concerning', آن کس = 'that person', که = relative 'who/that'). تریاک دارد is a relative predicate 'has opium' or 'brings opium' (دارد = 'has/possesses'). به شهر ('to/in the town') indicates the location affected. The whole clause specifies the person mentioned earlier: the man who has opium in the city.
segments.s650-g33: بدیشان (badishān) is a compound prepositional/oblique phrase meaning 'to them' and comes before the verb as the indirect object. چنین (chenīn) is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used transitively) — its lexical senses in the provided list do not match the verbal use, so treat it as the simple past 'he said'. کز is the contracted form of 'که از/که' meaning 'that' (introducing the reported clause). شهریار is the noun 'sovereign/king' and is the object/topic of the reported speech.
segments.s650-g34: سخن کردم is a compound verb: سخن (speech/talk) + کردن (to do) where کردم is the 1st-person past 'I spoke' or 'I addressed'. از here functions as 'about/concerning' (introducing manner/means), followed by هر دری 'هر' (every/each) modifying دری (here 'door/way' in the idiom 'از هر دری' = 'by every way'); دری's literal lexical senses in the list don't include 'door/way', so its sense is not in the provided options. خواستار is an adjective/participle meaning 'seeking/desirous' or 'a petitioner' describing the speaker's attitude.
segments.s650-g35: بباشید is the polite/plural imperative of بودن 'be/stay' (2nd-person plural/formal). تا is a subordinator meaning 'until'. بامداد and پگاه are emphatic paired nouns meaning 'dawn/morning' (both indicate early morning), placed together for poetic emphasis.
segments.s650-g36: نمایم is a 1st-person singular form of نشانیدن/نمودن 'to show' used here as 'I will show' (classical Persian uses this present/subjunctive form to express a near future or intention). شما is the 2nd-person plural/formal object 'you', and را is the definite object marker attaching to شما. سوی is the preposition 'toward', داد is the noun 'justice' modifying راه 'path/way' (داد راه = 'the path of justice'), and راه is the head noun 'road/way'.
segments.s650-g37: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past of رفتن 'they went/they departed'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. شبگیر here functions as a noun/adjective related to 'night' (literally 'night-visitor' or 'of the night') and is the subject of the following verb. باز is an adverb 'again' and آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they came/returned'. The clause therefore links departure with a subsequent nocturnal return.
segments.s650-g38: This segment lists two nominative items (شخوده رخ and پرگداز) joined by و, followed by the verb آمدند (they came/arrived) placed after the subjects — a common poetic word order. شخوده and پرگداز are presented as proper names/epithets here (lexical senses given are their literal noun meanings), رخ is 'face/appearance', and آمدند is again the 3rd-person plural past.
segments.s650-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. مزدک is the proper name 'Mazdak' as subject. ز is the poetic/prepositional form of 'از' meaning 'from/of' and here combines with در ('door/gate') — در is 'gate/door' (sense 1 in the list). آن modifies گره 'that knot' (گره = 'knot/problem'). را is the object marker attached to گره. بدید is the 3rd-person past 'he saw' (from دیدن).
segments.s650-g40: ز again is 'from', درگه is 'royal court' (the court/portal of the king), سوی is the preposition 'toward', شاه is 'king', ایران is the place-name 'Iran', and دوید is the 3rd-person past of دویدن 'he ran/ran hastily' — the clause describes Mazdak's swift movement from the court toward the king.
segments.s650-g233: This is a nominal clause with the theme (جهان) first, followed by the cause introduced by the preposition ز. پاک here is adverbial ('utterly/entirely') modifying ویران, and شود is the present/subjunctive/optative form of شدن used to express a potential or feared outcome ('would become / would be ruined'). Word order (Topic — cause — modifier — predicate) is typical poetic Persian.
segments.s650-g234: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it should not / must not (be the case)'; that (که) introduces the subordinate clause stating the forbidden outcome. این بد is 'this evil / misfortune' as the subject of the subordinate clause, به ایران gives the target ('to / upon Iran'), and شود again expresses occurrence ('happen / befall').
segments.s650-g235: همه is the universal subject 'everyone'; کدخدایند is the copular plural form ('they are headmen/chief men'). The coordinating و links the rhetorical question مزدور کی ست? — here کی ... ست places the interrogative 'who' before the copula, a common Persian question order.
segments.s650-g236: Parallel structure to the previous segment: همه 'everyone' + predicate (گنج دارند 'have treasure'). The second clause uses گنجور (here functioning as a noun/adjective 'one who possesses treasure / treasurer') followed by the interrogative کی and the copula ست, again forming a rhetorical question.
segments.s650-g237: ز دین‌آوران is a poetic partitive/agent-like phrase 'from / among the religion-bringers'. این سخن is the object/topic 'this statement', and کس نگفت is a negative assertion 'no one said (it)'. Note: نگفت is the past negative of گفتن 'to say'.
segments.s650-g238: تو is the second-person subject 'you'. دیوانگی 'madness' is the direct object or predicate noun, داشتی is the second-person singular past of داشتن 'you had / you harbored', در 'in/within', and نهفت is an adjective/participle 'hidden, concealed' — together 'you had hidden madness inside you' (i.e., you harbored madness).
segments.s650-g239: همه مردمان را is a topicalized direct object phrase 'all the people' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). به دوزخ gives direction/goal 'to hell'. The final word بری is a verbal form expressing action toward that goal; its exact lexical senses given in the list do not match the verbal sense in context, so the precise dictionary sense is not provided here.
segments.s650-g240: همی is an intensifying/focus particle ('indeed/just' or continuous marker), کار بد را is 'the bad deed' (را marking it as definite/emphasized), به بد نشمری is a negative imperative/present subjunctive construction meaning 'do not reckon/consider it bad' — نشمری is the negative of شمردن/شمردن as 'to count/consider', used here as 'do not consider'.
segments.s650-g121: This is a verbal clause in which the impersonal/patient phrase 'بر او شد' literally means 'came upon him' or 'befell him' (بر + شد = 'happened to/arrived at him'), followed by a relative clause 'آن کس که درویش بود' = 'that person who was a dervish.' Word order is verb (شد) after locative/experiential preposition (بر او) — typical of epic/archaic patterns.
segments.s650-g122: وگر = 'and/if/though' introducing a concessive clause; 'نانش' is the possessed noun 'his bread/livelihood' (literal bread = livelihood), 'از کوشش خویش' = 'from his own exertion' where خوی + ش forms the reflexive/possessive 'his own' and 'کوشش' is the source. 'بود' is simple past 'was.' The clause states that his sustenance came from his own labor.
segments.s650-g123: Literal order 'به گرد جهان تازه شد دین او' = 'the religion of him became new around the world.' 'به گرد جهان' = 'around the world' (preposition + noun phrase), 'تازه شد' = past of 'become new' (شد = become), and 'دین او' is the possessed noun 'his religion.' The verb follows the adverbial phrase — typical Persian order.
segments.s650-g124: This clause expresses inability/failure to find anyone: 'نیارست' is an older/poetic negative-past meaning 'was not found / there did not appear' (here meaning 'no one could be found'); it governs the infinitive 'جستن' ('to seek'). The phrase 'کسی کین او' = 'someone to take vengeance on him' (کین = vengeance/hatred, او = him). Altogether: 'no one could be found to seek his vengeance' (i.e. no one sought revenge against him).
segments.s650-g125: توانگر ('the wealthy') is the subject; همی is an archaic/poetic adverbial marker often used with past tense verbs to indicate continued or habitual action. 'سر ز تنگی نگاشت' literally 'he lowered/bent his head from narrowness/distress' — 'ز تنگی' = 'from distress/straiteness' and نگاشت here is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'bent/lowered' (not the common senses of 'write' in the lexicon), so the clause means the rich man also bowed his head in hardship.
segments.s650-g126: سپردی (here past 'he handed/entrusted') + 'به درویش' = 'to the dervish.' 'چیزی که داشت' is a relative clause 'something that he had/possessed.' The verb سپردی is simple past transferring action, and داشت is past possession 'had.'
segments.s650-g127: چنان بد که is a fixed poetic/consequential formula 'so (it happened) that...' introducing the following incident. 'یک روز پگاه' = 'one day at dawn' (یک روز = one day/one morning; پگاه = dawn/early morning). The phrase prepares the scene for the clause that follows.
segments.s650-g128: 'ز خانه بیامد' = 'he came from the house' (ز = from, خانه = house, بیامد = came, past). 'به نزدیک شاه' = 'to near the king' (= to the king's presence). The verb بیامد is third-person past 'came.'
segments.s650-g297: This is a compact nominative–object–verb clause in classical Persian order: 'سر پادشاهیش را کس ندید' literally 'the head of his kingship (را) nobody saw.' 'سر پادشاهی' (head of kingship) is the definite object marked by 'را', 'کس' functions as an indefinite pronoun in a negative clause ('no one' / 'nobody'), and 'ندید' is the past-tense verb with the negative prefix 'ن-' (did not see).
segments.s650-g298: This clause uses the past (perfective) verb 'نشد' (negated 'شد', 'did not become') followed by the predicative adjective 'خوار' ('despised'). 'هرکس که' is a relative phrase 'whoever/each person who', 'او را' marks the person chosen as definite object, and 'گزید' is simple past 'chose.' The whole line means 'whoever chose him did not become despised.'
segments.s650-g299: Relative clause 'هر آن کس که بینید خط قباد' = 'whoever/whichever person that sees Qobad's handwriting.' 'هر آن کس' is a formula meaning 'whoever,' 'که' introduces the relative clause, 'بینید' is present tense 'sees' (classical/plural form used generically), and 'خط قباد' is the object ('Qobad's script/handwriting').
segments.s650-g300: 'به جز پند کسری مگیرید یاد' is an admonitory general statement: 'take to heart no remembrance except Kisra's counsel.' The phrase 'به جز' functions as the idiom 'except,' 'پند کسری' is 'Kisra's advice,' 'مگیرید' is the present plural form 'you take' used here as a general injunction, and 'یاد' ('remembrance'/'memory') is the object meaning 'remember / take to heart.'
segments.s650-g301: This clause is first-person past 'سپردم' ('I entrusted') with the indirect object 'به کسری' ('to Kisra') and the predicate 'سزاوار تخت' ('deserving of the throne'). Word order places the recipient ('به کسری') before the verb and the adjectival phrase 'سزاوار تخت' after the verb to qualify the person entrusted.
segments.s650-g302: 'پس از مرگ ما' is a temporal prepositional phrase 'after our death' (here 'پس' = 'after'), followed by the copular clause 'او بود نیک‌بخت' = 'he was fortunate.' 'بود' is the past copula 'was', and 'نیک‌بخت' is an adjectival/predicative noun 'fortunate.'
segments.s650-g303: This is an optative / blessing clause introduced by 'که' ('that/which' here linking the wish), with 'یزدان' (God) as subject and 'خشنود باد' meaning 'may (he) be pleased.' The prepositional 'از این پور' specifies the object of God's pleasure 'with regard to this son.' 'باد' is the optative particle meaning 'may.'
segments.s650-g304: Parallel optative: 'دل بدسگالش پر از دود باد' = 'may the heart of his bad‑fated one be full of smoke.' 'دل' is subject 'heart,' 'بدسگالش' qualificative (here treated as 'ill‑fated' / hostile), 'پر از' is the predicative expression 'full of,' 'دود' is 'smoke,' and 'باد' again marks the optative 'may.'
segments.s650-g273: This clause uses a poetic/inverted word order. فرمود is the past-tense verb 'commanded/ordered'; کسری (Khosrow) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic effect. یکی دار ... بلند together mean 'a tall gallows' (یکی = 'a certain/one', دار = 'gallows', بلند = 'tall'), with the adjective بلند separated by inversion.
segments.s650-g274: فروهشت is a perfect/simple past verbal or participial idea meaning 'spread out' or 'was spread' placed before its source. از دار = 'from the gallows' names the origin, and پیچان کمند is a noun phrase 'twisting/noose' with پیچان (twisting) modifying کمند (noose/snare). Together: 'he spread twisting nooses from the gallows.'
segments.s650-g275: نگون‌بخت ('the ill‑fated/ill‑luck one') is marked by را as the definite direct object. زنده ('alive') modifies the object and sits before the verbal phrase بر دار کرد ('placed on the gallows'); کرد is the simple past 'did/caused (to be)'. So: 'He placed the ill‑fated man alive upon the gallows.'
segments.s650-g276: سر ('head') headed a nominal phrase modified by مرد بی‌دین ('the impious man'); نگون‌سار is a predicate adjective meaning 'ill‑fated/downcast' and کرد is the past-tense causative/predicative verb 'made/caused'. The line thus says he brought an ill‑fated state upon the impious man's head (poetically: doomed the impious man).
segments.s650-g277: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / from then on' (از + آن + پس). بکشتش is the past-tense verb 'he killed him' (ب+کشت+ش, direct object enclitic). به باران تیر is an instrumental/figurative expression 'by/with a rain (shower) of arrows' (به = by/with, باران تیر = 'rain of arrows').
segments.s650-g278: تو‌گر is the conditional 'if you...' (تو + گر). باهشی is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person form meaning 'you feel/are (compassionate/pitying)' (a subjunctive/conditional sense). راه مزدک مگیر is an imperative admonition: راه ('way/path') مزدک (Mazdak) مگیر = 'do not take Mazdak's path', where مگیر is the negative imperative 'do not take'.
segments.s650-g279: بزرگان ('the great ones' / nobles) is the subject. شدند is the past plural of شدن 'became'. ایمن از خواسته is a predicate meaning 'safe from desire' (ایمن = safe/protected, از = from, خواسته = 'desire/wish'). The clause states that the nobles became protected from desire.
segments.s650-g280: This segment lists the objects of that abstention: زن و زاده و باغ آراسته = 'women (or wives) and offspring and adorned gardens.' و is the coordinating 'and', زاده = 'offspring/children', باغ آراسته = 'adorned/tended garden' (آراسته modifies باغ). The nouns are simple coordinated complements of 'خواسته'.
segments.s650-g65: دویدند is a 3rd-person plural past verb 'they ran/they hastened.' Here it starts the clause and is followed by the relative phrase introduced by هرکس که — 'هرکس' = 'each person / whoever' and که = 'who/that' — so the phrase reads 'they ran — each one who was very hungry.' بد گرسنه is an idiomatic adjectival phrase where بد functions as an intensifier 'badly/very' modifying گرسنه 'hungry.'
segments.s650-g66: به here marks the action-target ('into/for'); تاراج is a noun 'pillage' used with کرد(ند) to form the action 'did pillage' (a common classical construction: noun + کردن). گندم is the object 'wheat.' شدند (شدند) is the past plural of شدن 'to become / to be done' used here with the noun to mean 'they plundered / they set about pillaging.' از بنه is a prepositional phrase 'from the heaps/piles' (بنه = pile/heap/store).
segments.s650-g67: چه ... چه ... is a correlative construction meaning 'whether ... or ... / both ... and ...'. انبار شهری is a noun phrase 'the city’s granary' (انبار + adjectival/possessive شهری), and آن قباد is 'that Qobad' (آن a demonstrative before the proper name). The two nouns are the two coordinated items of the correlative.
segments.s650-g68: ز is the poetic/formal preposition 'from / owing to'; یک‌دانه گندم is the measure/amount 'a single grain of wheat' (یک‌دانه = one seed/grain). نبودند is the past negative plural 'they were not' (نبودند = were not / there was not), and شاد is the predicate adjective 'glad/happy.' Together it means 'they were not glad because there was not even one grain of wheat.'
segments.s650-g69: چو دیدند is a temporal clause 'when they saw' (چو = when; دیدند = they saw). رفتند is the main past verb 'they went.' کارآگهان here is an adverbial/participial word meaning 'having been informed / at once / promptly' (literally 'the ones aware' used adverbially), so the sequence is 'When they saw (it), they at once went.'
segments.s650-g70: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to the vicinity/presence of.' بیدار is an epithet/adjective 'awake, vigilant' qualifying شاه جهان 'king of the world' (شاه = king; جهان = world). The entire phrase means 'to the presence/court of the vigilant king of the world.'
segments.s650-g71: که introduces an indirect statement/clause 'that ...'. تاراج کرد(ند) is the verb phrase built from the noun تاراج plus کردن — here rendered 'they pillaged / they did pillage.' انبار شاه is the object/affected thing 'the king's granary' (انبار + شاه in the genitive relationship). The clause reports what was said/reported to the king.
segments.s650-g72: به مزدک is the prepositional phrase 'to Mazdak' indicating the target/recipient of an action. همی‌بازگردد is a present/imperfective verb with the progressive prefix همی- plus بازگردد 'turn back/return' — here passive/impersonal in sense: 'the blame returns / is being turned.' گناه is the subject 'blame / fault' (literally 'sin') that is said to 'turn back to Mazdak.' Word order places the indirect object phrase before the verb and finishes with the subject noun.
segments.s650-g265: به مزدک is a prepositional phrase marking the indirect object ("to Mazdak"). چنين گفت کسری is poetically inverted word order: گفت is the past-tense verb "said," کسری is the subject (Kisra), and چنين is an adverb "thus." که introduces the reported speech (a complement clause). رو here is the verb root meaning "turn/go toward," functioning as the beginning of the imperative/subjunctive clause that follows.
segments.s650-g266: به درگاه باغ گرانمایه شو is the content of the reported command. به درگاه = "to/toward the threshold/portal" (درگاه here a physical portal). باغ گرانمایه = "the noble/precious garden," where the adjective گرانمایه follows the noun (poetic order). شو is the (hortative/imperative) form of شدن used here as a second-person command roughly "be/stand/go" — idiomatically: "go/turn to the portal."
segments.s650-g267: درختان ببین is an imperative: درختان (direct object) + ببین (2nd-person singular imperative of دیدن) = "Look at the trees." آنکه introduces a relative clause modifying درختان ("which/that"). هر کس ندید literally "every person did not see," functioning idiomatically as an existential negation meaning "that no one saw." Note the past tense ندید.
segments.s650-g268: نه is a negation here meaning "not/nor" (reinforcing the previous idea of no one having seen them). از کاردانان پیشین is a prepositional phrase "from the former/expert ones," where کاردانان is plural "the skilled/experts" and پیشین is an adjective "former/ancient." شنید is simple past "heard"; together with نه it means "nor have (they) been heard of by the earlier experts."
segments.s650-g269: بشد here is a narrative past/archaic form meaning roughly "he went/left" or "it came to pass; he departed." مزدک is the subject. از باغ is the source phrase "from the garden." و coordinates the next clause. بگشاد در is an older past form (بـ- prefix + گشاد) meaning "he opened" and در here means "door/gate." The clause as a whole: "Mazdak departed from the garden and opened the gate."
segments.s650-g270: که is the complementizer/purpose marker linking the previous action to its intention/result ("so that/that"). بیند is present-subjunctive or optative of دیدن ("may see/to see"), giving the purpose: "in order to see." مگر here means "except/only" (i.e., "sees nothing but"). بر + چمن بارور = "on the fertile meadow" with بارور ("fertile/fruitful") postposed to the noun. The clause expresses that he sees only the fertile lawn.
segments.s650-g271: همانگه is an adverbial phrase "at that moment/instant." که introduces the temporal clause "when he saw." دید is past tense "he saw." از تنش رفت هوش is a light-verb construction with object first: از تنش = "from his body" (تن + possessive clitic ش = "his"), رفت هوش = "awareness/sense went/left" i.e. "his consciousness left his body." Note the inversion where هوش (the thing that left) comes after the verb رفت.
segments.s650-g272: برآمد is past tense "arose/issued forth." به ناکام is an adverbial phrase meaning "in vain/fruitlessly" (به + ناکام). ز is the poetic preposition "from," and the following و in the text appears as part of the clitic sequence yielding "from him" (زو = ز + او), so together they mark source "from him." یک خروش = "a cry/uproar" (یک = one/a, خروش = cry). The whole: "a fruitless/ineffectual cry rose from him."
segments.s650-g161: همی is an archaic/progressive adverb meaning 'continually' or 'keeps on' and precedes the verb. دیو is the subject 'the demon'; پیچد is a present/past-form verb 'turns/turns about' (literary verb form); سر is the object 'head' placed before the verb in this elevated SOV word order; بخردان (ب + خردان) is a dative/oblique plural 'to/for the wise' marking the target/direction of the turning.
segments.s650-g162: بباید is a modal auxiliary 'one must/it is necessary' and takes a following verbal or infinitival element; نهاد here functions as the lexical verb 'place/put' (literary form related to نهادن) — together meaning 'must place'. این دو is the direct object 'these two', and اندر میان is a prepositional phrase 'in the middle / between' indicating where they must be placed.
segments.s650-g163: چو ... شد is a temporal clause 'when this was said' (چو = 'when', گفته شد = 'it was said', with شد as auxiliary/past). دست کسری گرفت is the main clause: گرفت is the past verb 'seized/took', and دست کسری ('Kisra's hand' or 'the hand of Kisra') is the noun phrase connected to that action — idiomatically 'Kisra seized (someone) by the hand' or 'Kisra laid hold'.
segments.s650-g164: بدو is a short form of 'به او' 'to him / at it' pointing back to the referent; مانده is a past participial sense 'was left / remained' (result state). بد here is an adverbial/intensifying use (archival/poetic) shading 'badly' or 'greatly' and modifies شگفت; شاه ایران is the subject 'the king of Iran'; شگفت is a predicate adjective 'astonished/amazed.'
segments.s650-g165: از او can be read as 'from him / at him' marking the target or source; نامور is an epithet 'the famed/renowned one' identifying the person; دست بستد is a compound verb 'بستد' (past) with دست as its object/part — literally 'closed/withdrew the hand' or 'made a fist / withdrew support'; به خشم is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'in anger'. The clause uses literary word order and a compact verb form بستد for past action.
segments.s650-g166: به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'swiftly / with haste' (به + تندی). ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of / from', so ز مزدک = 'because of Mazdak'. بخوابید is the past of خوابیدن 'fell asleep' (used here idiomatically, possibly euphemistic), and چشم 'eye' is the grammatical subject of that verb — a poetic inversion puts the noun before the verb.
segments.s650-g167: به مزدک is the indirect object 'to Mazdak' (به + proper name). چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'so/ thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); خندان qualifying قباد is a postposed descriptive phrase 'Qobad, smiling' (adjective before the name but functioning as a nominative phrase). The sentence order is literary: indirect object + report + subject/epithet.
segments.s650-g168: که introduces the quoted question (here equivalent to a complementizer introducing direct speech). از دین کسری is a genitive/oblique phrase 'concerning Kisra's religion/faith' (از = 'about/concerning', دین = 'religion', کسری = 'Kisra'). چه داری به یاد؟ is an idiomatic question 'what do you have in memory?' = 'what do you remember/what do you have in mind?' where داری is present 'do you have' and به یاد is the prepositional noun phrase 'in memory'.
segments.s650-g177: This clause begins with the indirect‑object adverb 'بدو' (to him), followed by the reporting verb 'گفت' (said) and the speaker name 'مزدک'. 'زمان چند روز' is a temporal phrase literally 'time how many days' functioning as the content of the question (how long). Word order is typical of Persian narrative: indirect object → reporting verb → subject → temporal phrase.
segments.s650-g178: 'همی‌خواهی' is 2nd‑person present (here rendered as a question: 'do you want/wish'). 'از شاه' means 'from the king' and the epithet 'گیتی‌فروز' follows 'شاه' as a descriptive apposition. The phrase as a whole is the direct question reported by the previous clause.
segments.s650-g179: 'ورا' = 'to him' (indirect object); 'گفت' is the past reporting verb 'said' (here introducing Kisra's reply). 'کسری' is the speaker (Kisra). 'زمان پنج ماه' is a temporal noun phrase 'a time/period of five months' and serves as the first part of the answer.
segments.s650-g180: 'ششم را' marks 'the sixth' with the object marker 'را' (here topicalizing the sixth month). 'همه' is the object 'everything/all', and 'بازگویم' is 1st‑person present/subjunctive used to express a future or intent ('I will recount/tell'). 'به شاه' closes the clause as the indirect object 'to the king'.
segments.s650-g181: 'بر این برنهادند' is a past plural verb phrase meaning 'they set on/upon this' or 'they adopted this (course)'; 'بر' + 'این' = 'upon this'. The conjunction 'و' links the clauses, and 'گشتند باز' = 'they turned back / returned', with 'باز' (again) emphasizing the return.
segments.s650-g182: The prepositional phrase 'به ایوان' ('to the iwan/porch') is followed by the verb 'بشد', an archaic past form meaning 'it came to be / it happened / he came'. The subject 'شاه گردن‌فراز' ('the proud/high‑necked king') follows the verb, a common poetic inversion placing the verb before the subject.
segments.s650-g183: 'فرستاد' is the past transitive verb 'sent/dispatched' with the agent 'کسری' following it. The phrase 'به هر جای کس' ('to every place, any person') uses 'هر' + 'جای' to mean 'every place' and 'کس' ('anyone') as the patient/recipient group; the exact scope is clarified by the following relative clause.
segments.s650-g184: The relative pronoun 'که' introduces a clause modifying 'کس'. 'داننده‌ای' (with the indefinite suffix -ای) means 'one who knows / a knowledgeable person' and 'دید' is the past 'he saw' (i.e., 'whom he saw to be'). 'و فریادرس' is appended as an additional quality 'and a rescuer/helper', so the whole clause qualifies the people Kisra sent.
segments.s650-g89: شکم گرسنه چند مردم بمرد is a compact, somewhat poetic clause: شکم گرسنه (literally 'hungry belly') is a nominal phrase describing hungry people; چند here functions as an indefinite/quantifying word ('several' / 'many') modifying مردم. بمرد is a verbal form meaning 'perished/died' used poetically (the b‑ prefix is common in older Persian verb forms). Word order is topical: descriptive phrase + quantifier + people + verb.
segments.s650-g90: که introduces the causal clause here ('because' / 'for'). انبار را uses را as the definite/object marker pointing to 'the granary' as topic of the remainder. سود جانش literally 'benefit to his life' (سود = benefit; جانش = his life). نبرد in this position functions as a negative past/resultative sense 'did not bring / did not avail' (classical poetic usage of بردن with negative), so the clause says the granary did not benefit their life.
segments.s650-g91: ز گفتار او 'from his speech' uses ز (poetic form of از) to mark cause/origin. گفتار او is the genitive construction 'his speech'. تنگ‌دل شد قباد follows: تنگ‌دل (adjective 'narrow‑hearted'/'close‑minded') + شد ('became') + قباد (proper name) — note the name appears after the predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s650-g92: بشد here is a past/resultative sense of شدن with the prefixed b‑ ('it became' / 'it happened'). تیز مغزش is an adjectival phrase 'his brain sharp' (تیز modifying مغز), with مغزش 'his brain' (possession by suffix). ز گفتار داد again gives the cause ('from the speech of داد'); داد is a noun meaning 'justice' here, so the clause reads 'his brain became sharp from the speech of justice.' The verb precedes/frames the adjectival predicate in poetic inversion.
segments.s650-g93: وز آن پس is a temporal connective 'after that' (وز here is a poetic variant of از). بپرسید is a past tense form 'he asked' and شنید is the past 'he heard', with و + پاسخ between them 'and (he) heard an answer'. Word order is straightforward subject implied by the verb forms (third person).
segments.s650-g94: دل و جان او پر ز گفتار دید is a perceptive clause: دل و جان او 'his heart and life' is the theme; پر ز گفتار 'full of speech' is a predicate nominal (پر = 'full (of)'); دید ('he saw' / 'he perceived') functions as the verb governing the state — literally 'he saw his heart and life full of speech.' The order places the possessed nouns before the predicate, common in poetry.
segments.s650-g95: ز چیزی که گفتند پیغمبران is a relative/cause phrase: ز چیزی 'from something' + که introduces the relative clause 'that گفتند پیغمبران' ('that the prophets said'). Note the poetic V–S order where گفتند (said) appears before پیغمبران (prophets).
segments.s650-g96: همان is a demonstrative 'that very' / 'the same'. دادگر qualifies the following nouns as 'just' or 'righteous' and appears before the coordinated plural موبدان و ردان (mōbeds and ردان). This is an attributive adjective + coordinated noun phrase; the final term ردان is archaic/less certain in meaning and left untranslated here as a preserved term.
segments.s650-g329: به سر شد کنون داستان قباد is an inverted poetic order meaning “the story of Qobad has come to an end now.” به سر شدن (literally “to come to the head/top”) is an idiom meaning “to finish/come to an end.” شد is the past/perfective verb 'became/was' and کنون = 'now' places the action in the present-past viewpoint.
segments.s650-g330: ز کسری کنم ز این سپس نام یاد is a sequence of announcements: ز + کسری = “of/about Kisra,” کنم is first-person present/future (‘I will do/say’), and the second phrase (ز این سپس نام یاد) indicates 'after this (ز این سپس) I will mention/recall the name (نام یاد)'. The first ز is the poetic preposition 'of/about', کنم is the speaker's promise to narrate, and the second z-phrase marks the temporal sequence.
segments.s650-g331: همش داد بود و همش رای و نام lists the ruler's attributes: همش ('all of it' / 'entirely') + داد ('justice') + بود (past 'was'), followed by another coordinate list همش رای و نام ('all was counsel and renown'). The adjective-like nouns are predicated with بود; word order places emphasis on داد (justice) first.
segments.s650-g332: به داد و دهش یافته نام و کام is a causal/instrumental clause: به here functions as 'by/through' (instrumental), داد و دهش = 'justice and giving/generosity', یافته is a past participle 'having obtained' / 'acquired', and نام و کام are the results 'fame and fulfillment'. The construction literally: 'By justice and gifts (he) has obtained name and satisfaction.'
segments.s650-g333: الا‌ای دلارای سرو بلند is a vocative address. الا‌ای functions as an exclamation/vocative 'O!' or 'behold, O', دلارای = 'beloved' (term of endearment), سرو بلند = 'tall cypress' (a poetic epithet for a beautiful tall beloved). The order is typical Persian vocative stacking.
segments.s650-g334: چه بودت که گشتی چنین مستمند؟ is an interrogative of circumstance: چه بودت ('what had befallen you' / 'what was to you') with بودت = 2nd-person past copula, که introduces the result/clause, گشتی is past of گشتن used here inchoatively 'you became' (classical usage), چنین = 'so/thus', and مستمند = predicate adjective 'needy/poor'. Together: 'What happened to you that you became so needy?'
segments.s650-g335: بدان شادمانی و آن فر و زیب is a prepositional/causal phrase: بدان (here 'by/for/on account of that' — in classical Persian often written as one word) + شادمانی 'rejoicing' and آن فر و زیب 'that farr (royal splendour) and beauty'. It sets the cause or reference for the following question.
segments.s650-g336: چرا شد دل روشنت پرنهیب؟ is a direct question: چرا 'why', شد (past of شدن) = 'did ... become', دل روشن = 'your bright/clear heart' (دل + adjectival روشن), and پرنهیب is a predicate meaning 'full of dread/awesome/formidable' (poetic). The verb شد marks a change of state: 'why did your bright heart become (so) ...?'
segments.s650-g105: جهان راست is a topical/claused phrase meaning 'it is right in the world' (literal 'world true'). باید که باشد is a modal clause: باید (must/ought) + که باشد (subjunctive 'that there be/should be') expressing what ought to obtain. به چیز is a prepositional phrase 'with/regarding something' indicating the domain in which that state (being abundant) should hold.
segments.s650-g106: فزونی توانگر is a nominal phrase 'the increase/excess of the wealthy' (فزونی as head, توانگر as possessor). چرا جست نیز is a past-tense question: چرا (why) + جست (3rd pers. past of جستن 'sought') with نیز 'also/still' adding emphasis — the implied subject of جست is توانگر.
segments.s650-g107: This is a simple nominal list (زن و خانه و چیز) followed by بخشیدنی‌ست, an adjective/verbal-nominal form + copula meaning 'is giveable / may be given'. The construction states these items are things that can be parted with or bestowed.
segments.s650-g108: تهی‌دست کس 'the empty-handed/poor person' is the subject; با توانگر یکی‌ست is an equative predicate 'is one/identical with the wealthy' (با = 'with', یکی‌ست = 'is the same'). The line asserts an equality or likeness between poor and rich in the poet's point.
segments.s650-g109: من این را کنم uses من (I) + این را (this as definite object marked by را) + کنم (1st pers. subjunctive/volitive of کردن, here expressing intention or future-like action 'I will do'). راست modifies the verb ('do rightly'). با دین پاک is an instrumental phrase 'by/with pure faith'.
segments.s650-g110: شود is an impersonal/3rd-person subjunctive or future-like 'it will become / it shall be'; ویژه پیدا بلند is a string of modifiers meaning 'especially apparent and lofty' (ویژه 'special/especially', پیدا 'visible/manifest', بلند 'high/lofty'). از مغاک 'out of the abyss' gives the origin or source from which it will be raised or appear.
segments.s650-g111: هر آن کس که is a standard relative phrase 'whoever/anyone who'; او is the stressed/he subject pronoun; جز بر این دین بود combines جز بر 'except/on other than' + این دین 'this religion/faith' + بود (past 'was'), so the clause means 'whoever was not of this faith'.
segments.s650-g112: ز یزدان وز منش is a poetic coordination meaning 'from God and from (one's) nature/character' — note وز here is a contracted poetic form (و ز = 'and from'). نفرین بود is the predicate 'was a curse / was accursed' (بود past). The whole segment says such a person was accursed by God and by nature/character.
segments.s650-g25: سزای چنین مرد is a genitive construction: 'the punishment/desert of such a man.' گویی here is an archaic/poetic imperative/hortative 'say/tell' (literally 'you say' or 'tell'), introducing the subordinate clause that begins with که; چیـست is the present copula 'what is'. Word order is somewhat inverted for verse: noun-phrase + verb + complementizer + interrogative.
segments.s650-g26: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'مرد' ('who/that'). تریاک دارد is simple present 'has opium.' درم سنگ بیست is a numerical/measure phrase: درم (a dirham/coin) qualified by سنگ (here used as an attributive/measure word) followed by the numeral بیست 'twenty' — an archaic ordering where the measured noun and qualifier precede the numeral.
segments.s650-g27: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' داد پاسخ (literally 'gave answer') is a verb + object phrase; ورا is the clitic/old object pronoun 'to him' (or 'him') and شهریار, the subject 'the king', comes at the end — a poetic verb‑first (V ... S) order common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s650-g28: که here introduces reported speech ('that/that/that he said'). خونی‌ست is a predicative phrase: خونی (blood‑stained / bloody / bloodthirsty) + ست (copula 'is'). این مرد تریاک‌دار is the noun phrase 'this man, opium‑holder' where تریاک‌دار is a compound adjective 'one who has/carries opium.'
segments.s650-g29: به خون گزیده is a descriptive phrase: خون گزیده ('one who has tasted blood' or 'blood‑thirsty') is presented with the preposition به in front for emphasis. ببایدش is an impersonal/archaic modal construction 'it must be' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' attached. کشت is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to kill' / 'kill' used as the main lexical verb of obligation: 'he must be killed.'
segments.s650-g30: به درگاه is a locative phrase 'to/at the threshold (or in the royal presence).' چون here is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause چون دشمن آمد 'when an enemy came.' آمد is simple past 'came.' به مشت is an adverbial of manner 'with a fist' (به marking the instrument/manner). Word order is flexible/poetic: locative phrase may precede the temporal clause.
segments.s650-g31: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' بشنید is past tense 'he heard.' برخاست is the verb 'he rose' or 'stood up.' از پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from before the king / from the king's presence' where پیش means 'in the presence of,' and از marks motion away.
segments.s650-g32: بیامد is simple past 'he came.' به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to near/nearby' (he approached), and فریادخواه is the object 'the petitioner/one crying for help.' The order places the verb before the prepositional phrase and object, a normal poetic arrangement.
segments.s650-g353: خروشان شد is a compound predicate: خروشان (an adjective/participle meaning 'tumultuous/roaring') precedes شد (past-tense 'became') to mean 'became tumultuous.' آن نرگسان دژم is the subject phrase: آن 'that/those' + نرگسان (poetic plural referring to narcissi or, figuratively, beautiful eyes) with the postpositive adjective دژم 'sullen/grim.' Word order here puts the predicative phrase before the subject for poetic emphasis.
segments.s650-g354: همان سرو آزاده شد پشت خم: همان 'that/same' points back to a previously mentioned figure; سرو آزاده is a noun + epithet ('cypress' = tall/beautiful person, آزاده = 'noble/free-spirited'). شد is the past 'became' and is followed by the nominal result/backward phrase پشت خم ('back bent' / 'bent-backed'), so together: 'became with bent back' (i.e., it bent its back). The adjective آزاده functions as an epithet on سرو; پشت خم is the resulting state.
segments.s650-g355: دل شاد و بی‌غم is the topical subject ('the heart, happy and carefree'). پر از درد گشت is the predicate: پر از 'full of' + درد 'sorrow/pain' and گشت (past of گشتن) 'became/turned.' The adjective phrase precedes the verbal predicate, a normal Persian order in poetry: subject + predicative clause.
segments.s650-g356: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the clause. روز ما is a possessive noun phrase 'our day' (ما as possessor). ناجوانمرد is a predicative adjective meaning 'dishonourable/cowardly' and گشت is the past 'became/turned' — together 'our day became dishonourable.'
segments.s650-g357: بدانگه (thereupon/at that time) introduces a temporal clause; که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/when/which.' مردم is the subject 'the people/men,' شود is the subj./past-form 'become' (here 'become/symbolically turn into a state'), and سیر شیر is a noun phrase literally 'sated/full of milk' (سیر 'satiated', شیر here taken in this line as 'milk' in the phrase 'سیرِ شیر'). The clause thus means 'when the people became sated with milk.'
segments.s650-g358: شتاب آورد is a compound verb 'brought haste' = 'hastened/came quickly.' مرگ is the subject 'Death' (preceding the verb, common in poetry). خواندش is خواند + enclitic ش (him), here 'called him' (either 'named/summoned him' depending on context), and پیر is the complement 'old/elderly.' Together the clause reads 'Death hastened and called him old.'
segments.s650-g359: چل و هشت is the numeral 'forty-eight' (چل = forty, و = and, هشت = eight); in poetry numerals often appear before epithets. بد عهد is a compound adjective 'bad‑oath' = 'faithless/unreliable,' and نوشین روان is a poetic epithet 'sweet‑soul(ed)' (نوشین 'sweet/charming' + روان 'soul/spirit'). Together they form a descriptive phrase about the person and their age.
segments.s650-g360: تو is the direct address 'you.' بر شست رفتی is an idiomatic verbal phrase with رفتی (past 'you went/left'); بر شست functions adverbially here (in older poetic usage it can indicate a passing/away or sudden departure). نمانی جوان is a negative/resultative clause: نمانی (from ماندن) 'you did not remain/last' + جوان 'young' as the predicate complement, 'you did not remain young.'
segments.s650-g345: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by' introducing the source; سرو دلارای is a noun phrase 'the beloved/heart‑adorning cypress' (دلارای is an epithet attached to سرو). چنبر کند is a verb phrase with کند (third‑person singular present/simple) meaning 'makes/forms'; word order is source (ز + NP) → object (چنبر) → verb (کند), a common poetic inversion.
segments.s650-g346: سمن برگ را is the direct object phrase ('the jasmine leaf') marked by را. رنگ عنبر کند is the predicate: رنگ (here a noun used with کند as a result/quality) + عنبر specifying the color ('amber'), with کند again 'makes/causes to be'. The implied subject is the same agent as the previous clause; Persian allows repeating کند for parallel actions.
segments.s650-g347: گل ارغوان را is the object phrase 'the purple/crimson flower' (ارغوان specifying color). کند زعفران places کند after the object with زعفران as the result/quality: 'he makes (it) saffron-colored' — کند functions as the verb 'makes/dyes'.
segments.s650-g348: پس is an adverb meaning 'then/afterwards'. The clause that follows is elliptical (verb is omitted for poetic brevity): زعفران رنجهای گران links 'saffron' with 'heavy sufferings' — i.e. 'then saffron (that dye/condition) brings/means heavy sufferings.' The verb 'is/comes' is implied.
segments.s650-g349: شود is the auxiliary/finite verb 'becomes'. بسته is a predicative adjective/participle 'bound/tied'. بی‑بند پای is a compound adjective 'with unbound/free foot' modifying نوند (a musical mode or technical term); the whole phrase means 'the Navand that used to be free‑footed becomes bound' — adjective phrase + noun → predicate 'شود بسته'.
segments.s650-g350: وز او here is an archaic/poetic causal/agency phrase ('by/through him' or 'because of him'); خوار گردد is a passive/state expression 'becomes despised' (گردد = 'becomes'). تن ارجمند is the patient noun phrase 'the noble/esteemed body' (object of the state). Word order is prepositional causal phrase → predicate → patient.
segments.s650-g351: مرا is the object 'me' (here split in the provided tokens as م + را); در خوشاب is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in the pleasant water/drink'. سستی گرفت is a common idiom 'weakness/lassitude seized' (گرفت past tense 'seized/took'). The verb governs the object مرا, so the natural reading is 'weariness seized me in the pleasant water.'
segments.s650-g352: همان سرو آزاد is the subject phrase 'that same free/lofty cypress' (همان = 'that/the same', آزاد = 'free/high'). پستی گرفت repeats the idiom pattern with گرفت: 'took on baseness' or 'fell into lowliness' — پستی 'meanness/low state' + گرفت 'seized/assumed'. This is a short, forceful past construction typical of epic poetry.
segments.s650-g57: چنین داد پاسخ — a fixed poetic phrase: چنین ("such/thus") modifies داد پاسخ ("gave an answer"). Word order is Verb (داد) + object (پاسخ) with the demonstrative/qualifier چنین fronting the clause; که after it introduces the content of the reply. می‌کن is an imperative/performative here (sense: "do") and بنش is the imperative or causative notion of 'seat' ("make him sit"), so the sequence می‌کن بنش functions as a direct command inside the reported speech.
segments.s650-g58: که خونی‌ست ناکرده بر گردنش — that-clause giving the reason. خونی‌ست is a nominal predicate (خون + ـی + است) meaning roughly "there is blood / a blood-claim" (poetic for a blood-debt or murder). ناکرده is a negative past participle "not done/avenged" modifying خون. بر گردنش is a prepositional phrase "upon his neck," with the enclitic ش = "his." The whole clause: "for a blood (claim) has not been settled on his neck."
segments.s650-g59: چو بشنید مزدک زمین بوس داد — چو is a temporal conjunction "when." بشنید is the simple past "he heard." The subject (مزدک) follows the verb by poetic inversion. زمین بوس داد is a patterned expression: زمین ("earth") + بوس ("kiss") + داد ("gave") — literally "gave a kiss to the earth," i.e. he prostrated or kissed the ground; داد here is the simple past verb "gave" used with the noun بوس to form the action.
segments.s650-g60: خرامان بیامد ز پیش قباد — an independent clause describing his approach. خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb "with a stately/proud gait." بیامد is simple past "came." ز پیش قباد (literally "from before Qobad") is a poetic locative/agentive phrase meaning "into Qobad's presence" or "toward Qobad" (ز پیش = 'from before/in the presence of').
segments.s650-g61: به درگاه او شد به انبوه گفت — به درگاه او شد is a short verbal phrase meaning "he came/appeared in his (the king's) court/presence" (به = to, درگاه = royal court/presence, شد = past verb indicating arrival/occurrence). به انبوه گفت means "he spoke to the multitude" (انبوه = many/multitude; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' although the provided lexical senses list gives noun senses — the context requires the verb 'said/spoke'). Note the compact, poetic order: arrival clause then speech to the crowd.
segments.s650-g62: که جایی که گندم بود در نهفت — a nested relative clause introduced by که: "that there was a place where wheat had been hidden." جایی که = "a place where," گندم بود = "wheat was" (بود = past of 'to be'), and در نهفت is a postposed participial/locative phrase meaning "was concealed/hidden." Grammatically, نهفت is a past participle/adjective modifying the situation of the wheat (hidden).
segments.s650-g63: دهید آن به تاراج در کوی و شهر — دهید is the plural/formal imperative "give" (order to subordinates), آن = "that/it" (the thing to be given), به تاراج = "to pillage/plunder" (تاراج as verbal noun), and در کوی و شهر = locative phrase "in the lanes and the city." The whole is an imperative instruction: "Give it up to plunder in the alleys and the city."
segments.s650-g64: بدان تا یکایک بیابید بهر — بدَان here is the fused prepositional/verb element (ب + دان) functioning roughly as 'by that / with that' or 'do so,' followed by تا introducing purpose "so that/in order that." یکایک = "one by one," and بیابید is the subjunctive/imperative plural "you find/you may find." بهر = "for / to (someone's) due/advantage." Together: "Do this so that you may find each one, one by one, for their share."
segments.s650-g97: به + گفتار مزدک = a prepositional phrase meaning 'according to / in the speech of Mazdak'. Here به has the sense 'in / according to' and precedes the noun phrase (typical Persian order). همه کژ گشت is a short clause 'everything became crooked' where کژ (wrong/perverse) is the predicative adjective and گشت is the past intransitive 'became/turned'.
segments.s650-g98: سخنهاش is سخن‌ها + ـاش (possessive) = 'his sayings/speech'. ز اندازه اندر گذشت is an idiomatic poetic string: ز (from/beyond) + اندازه (measure/limit) + گذشت (passed/exceeded). اندر here helps form the verbal phrase 'گذشت' (transgressed/exceeded), so the whole segment means 'his words passed beyond measure'.
segments.s650-g99: بر او انجمن شد literally 'an assembly happened/upon him' — بر here has the adversative sense 'against' and precedes the pronoun او. انجمن شد uses شد ('happened/was formed') to indicate that an assembly came into being; فراوان سپاه postmodifies 'an assembly' as 'a large host'.
segments.s650-g100: بسی کس is an older/formal way to say 'many people' (here treated as a quantified NP). به بیراهی آمد is 'came to/into error' — به introduces the state/result and بیراهی is 'deviation/error'; آمد is simple past 'came' used intransitively. ز راه at the end 'from the way' (ز = 'from') explains the source they departed.
segments.s650-g101: همی‌گفت is the habitual/progressive past 'kept saying' (همی- marks continuous or repeated action). هر کاو is an archaic/poetic relative phrase equivalent to 'whoever' (هر + کاو), introducing the conditional/relative clause; توانگر بود is a past-tense predicate 'was wealthy'. Note: کاو here functions as a relative pronoun rather than its literal lexical senses.
segments.s650-g102: تهیدست با او برابر بود = 'the poor was equal with him.' با او 'with him' is the prepositional phrase marking the reference of equality; برابر is the predicate adjective 'equal' and بود is simple past 'was'. Word order is subject (تهیدست) → circumstantial (با او) → predicate (برابر بود).
segments.s650-g103: نباید که باشد کسی is an impersonal negative construction 'there should not be anyone' (نباید = 'should not / must not' in an impersonal sense; که introduces the subordinate clause and باشد is the present/subjunctival form 'be'). برفزود follows as a verb meaning 'be raised/exalted' or 'increased' — here referring to being exalted above others.
segments.s650-g104: توانگر بود تار و درویش پود is a parallel nominal statement using textile imagery: 'the wealthy was the تار (warp) and the درویش (poor) the پود (weft)'. بود is past copula 'was'; تار ... پود is a metaphorical apposition showing complementary roles. The conjunction و simply links the two noun phrases.
segments.s650-g289: ز introduces the temporal/original phrase ('from/after') and is followed by شاهیش, here meaning 'his kingship' (شاهی + enclitic). چون ... شد is a temporal clause 'when ... became': چون (here = 'when/since') + سال 'year' + شد 'became'; بر چهل marks the measure 'to forty' (reached forty). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal subordinate (ز شاهیش چون سال شد بر چهل) before the main clause.
segments.s650-g290: غم روز مرگ is a possessed noun phrase 'the grief/sorrow of the day of death' (روز modifies مرگ in genitive relation). اندر آمد به دل is a compound verb phrase 'came into the heart' with اندر آمد (came within) + به (into/towards) + دل (heart) — the verb آمد appears after the locative adverb اندر as in older/poetic word order.
segments.s650-g291: یکی نامه بنوشت is 'he wrote a letter' where یکی ('a certain' / one) modifies نامه before the verb بنوشت (an archaic/poetic past form 'wrote'); پس 'then' follows the verb and بر حریر is a prepositional phrase 'on silk'. Note بنوشت is an older poetic past-tense form of نوشتن.
segments.s650-g292: بر آن خط شایسته خود reads 'on that line/hand of fitting (style) his own' where بر آن = 'on that', خط = 'hand/script', شایسته is a postpositive adjective 'fitting/appropriate', and خود 'his own' emphasises possession. The final sequence 'بد دبیر' in the manuscript is a poetic/archaising form meaning essentially 'to the scribe' (i.e. بد = variant of به), so the whole segment says he put/gave it to the scribe in that appropriate hand of his.
segments.s650-g293: نخست 'first/at the outset' + آفرین کرد literally 'made praise' = 'praised'. کرد is the light verb 'did/made' combining with آفرین to mean 'expressed praise'. بر دادگر uses بر + agent noun to indicate the object/recipient 'to the just one' (دادگر = the just ruler/judge).
segments.s650-g294: که introduces the content clause ('that / namely'), دارد is present-tense 'has/possesses'. از او marks source 'from him' and links to the nouns دین 'religion/faith' and هنر 'art/skill'; the construction 'دارد از او دین و هم زو هنر' repeats the source (زو = از او contracted) to mean 'he has from him religion and likewise art' — a rhetorical duplication common in classical Persian.
segments.s650-g295: بباشد is an optative/subjunctive form 'may it be' or 'let it be', used to express a wish or formal supplication; همه بی‌گمان 'all without doubt' qualifies the wish. هرچه گفت is a free relative 'whatever he said' with گفت as the past verb 'said' (classical/poetic narration uses simple past here).
segments.s650-g296: The correlative pair چه ... و چه ... functions 'whether ... or ...': چه بر آشکار 'whether (in) the open' (بر + آشکار = openly), و چه اندر نهفت 'or (in) the hidden' (اندر = in/within, نهفت = hidden). This provides the two alternatives 'openly or secretly'.
segments.s650-g225: This is a rhetorical interrogative: 'چه' (here meaning 'how/what') introduces the question. 'داند' is the classical 3rd‑person present of 'دانستن' (knows); the noun phrase 'پسر کش' is a compound (پسر + agentive کش) meaning 'one who kills sons' (son‑killer). The clause 'که باشد پدر' is a subordinate relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that'), with 'باشد' functioning as the classical present/subjunctive copula 'is', so the whole line asks how the son‑killer could know who is a father.
segments.s650-g226: Subject 'پدر' (father) comes first, then the adverb 'همچنین' ('likewise/similarly'), and the interrogative 'چون' ('how') introduces the verb 'شناسد' (3rd‑person classical form of 'شناختن', 'recognize/know'). The object 'پسر' follows; the word order is Subject–Adverb–Interrogative/Verb–Object, producing 'How, then, would the father recognize the son?'.
segments.s650-g227: 'چو' here means 'when'; it introduces a temporal clause. 'مردم سراسر بود در جهان' uses 'بود' (past/old present of 'بودن') to state 'people were all over the world' — 'سراسر' is an adverb 'throughout/entire', and the prepositional phrase 'در جهان' ('in the world') follows the verb.
segments.s650-g228: 'نباشند' is a classical negative 3rd‑person plural form meaning 'they are not / there are not' (here used to state nonexistence/absence). 'پیدا' is predicate adjective 'visible/apparent'. The coordinated nouns 'کهان و مهان' name classes of notable people (literally 'the ancients' and 'the great/guests'); the sentence asserts that such figures were not to be seen.
segments.s650-g229: The first 'که' is a relative/indefinite 'who/that' introducing the clause. 'باشد' is the classical copula (here optative/potential sense 'would be' / 'could be'), and the second 'که' again introduces 'جوید' ('seeks' — classical present). The prepositional phrase 'در کهتری' means 'in inferiority/lowliness'; overall the clause asks rhetorically who would be the sort to seek in lowliness.
segments.s650-g230: 'چگونه' ('how') opens the question; 'توان' is the modal auxiliary 'can/able to'; 'یافتن' is the infinitive 'to find' (used here in the construction 'توان + infinitive' = 'can find'); 'مهتری' is the abstract noun 'preeminence/superiority' — the clause asks how one could possibly obtain or find superiority.
segments.s650-g231: 'کسی' is the indefinite 'anyone/someone' and 'کاو' here functions classically as a relative ('who' / 'that he'), introducing the description. The phrase 'مرد جای و چیزش' lists attributes — 'مرد' (manhood/valor), 'جای' (position/rank), and 'چیزش' (his possessions) — with the possessive suffix '-ش' attached to the last item, and 'که راست' predicates that these are 'right/intact'. The order is typical classical Persian: relative + list of nouns + predicate adjective.
segments.s650-g232: 'که' again introduces a relative clause; 'شد' is the past/preterite 'became'. The compound 'کارجو بنده' places two nouns in apposition — 'کارجو' (an applicant/petitioner seeking office) and 'بنده' (a servant/slave) — so 'شد کارجو بنده' = 'became a petitioner/servant'. The phrase 'با شاه راست' ('with the king' + 'راست') qualifies this state as occurring before the king or in relation to the king and reads as 'who became a petitioner and a (mere) servant in the king's presence/service.'
segments.s650-g113: ببد is an archaic/past-form of بودن (here translated “was”); it appears before the relative هرکه (’whoever’) in a verb‑initial, poetic order. درویش با او یکی is a noun phrase describing the person (’a dervish who was one with him / joined to him’), with با او (’with him’) immediately following the head noun and یکی functioning as an emphatic/indefinite modifier (‘one / a certain one’).
segments.s650-g114: The repeated اگر … اگر pattern renders a choice or concession: ‘whether … or …’. مرد بودند is past plural of بودن (بودند = ‘they were’), so the clause reads ‘if/when they were men’; کودکی here means ‘a child’. The second اگر links the alternative ‘or if (they were) a child’.
segments.s650-g115: از این here means ‘because of this / from this’ and links back to the preceding statement. بستدی is an old past form of بستن used transitively (‘he withheld / closed off’), with چیز as its object. و دادی (past of دادن) follows, ’and he gave’, and the following ب + دان is an older compacted form meaning ‘to that one’ (i.e. ‘gave it to him/that one’).
segments.s650-g116: فرو مانده is a compound participial phrase meaning ‘left down / abased / remained low’; مانده is the participle ‘left / remaining’. بد here functions adverbially (‘badly / poorly’). زان سخن = ‘because of that speech/remark’ (زان = از آن), and بخردان is a contraction of به خردان ‘in the eyes of / to the wise’ (i.e. ‘by the wise’).
segments.s650-g117: چو (’when’) introduces a temporal clause. بشنید is the past tense ‘he heard’. در دین او شد is a causative/ resultive expression with شد ‘became’ and the prepositional phrase در دین او ‘into his religion / a follower of his faith’; the proper name قباد (Qobad) appears at the end in typical poetic inversion and is the subject who underwent that change.
segments.s650-g118: ز گیتی here stands for ‘the world / mankind’ (گیتی) introduced in a poetic order; به گفتار او is a prepositional phrase ‘at/by his words / because of his speech’. بود شاد is the past copula plus adjective ‘was glad / rejoiced’. Together: ‘the world was glad at his words.’
segments.s650-g119: ورا is an object pronoun-like or directional adverb meaning ‘to him’ / ‘thereupon’; شاه is the agent ‘the king’. بنشاند is an archaic past form meaning ‘set / seated (someone)’ (i.e. ‘he seated him’). بر دست راست is the prepositional phrase ‘on the right hand’ (i.e. ‘at his right hand’), placed after the verb as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s650-g120: ندانست is ن- (negative) + دانست (past of دانستن), i.e. ‘did not know’. لشکر is the subject ‘the army’ (or ‘the host’). که introduces an indirect question/compliment clause and موبد کجاست (‘where the mobed is’) is the embedded interrogative (کجا ‘where’ + ست copula ‘is’).
segments.s650-g185: کس آمد سوی خره اردشیر is a simple past-clause: کس (indefinite pronoun) is the subject, آمد is the past 3rd-person singular of آمدن 'to come', and سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward/to'. خرِه اردشیر functions as a place-name phrase (خره followed by the proper name اردشیر) indicating the destination; word order is subject — verb — prepositional destination (typical Persian).
segments.s650-g186: که introduces a relative/adverbial clause giving a locational/explanatory comment ('where/for'), آنجا is 'there', بد describes the quality ('bad/poor'), از here is a preposition used in a comparative/relational sense ('from/of'), داد is the noun 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'), and هرمزد پیر is a noun phrase 'old Hormozd' (personal name + adjective). Together the clause says that at that place Hormozd's justice was bad; word order is typical adjective after noun for پیر modifying هرمزد.
segments.s650-g187: ز is the poetic/prepositional form of 'from', اصطخر is a place-name (Istakhr), مهرآذر is a personal name (Mehrāzar), and پارسی is an adjective 'Persian' modifying that name (noun-adjective order: name + epithet). The whole phrase gives origin and identification: 'from Istakhr, Mehrāzar the Persian'.
segments.s650-g188: بیامد is the past 3rd-person singular (came) in a slightly literary form, بدرگاه is a locative noun 'to the royal court/presence', با is the preposition 'with', and یار here means 'companion' (the following numeral سی, 'thirty', attaches to یار to indicate 'thirty companions'). The clause states movement to the court accompanied by companions.
segments.s650-g189: نشستند is a past tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they sat' (classical Persian of نشستن), دانش‌پژوهان is the plural subject 'scholars/students', به هم is a compound adverbial meaning 'together/with one another'. Word order here is verb — subject — adverbial (poetic variability), but the sense is 'the scholars sat together.'
segments.s650-g190: سخن is 'speech/talk', رفت is the past verb 'went' used idiomatically here ('talk went' = 'conversation proceeded/took place'), هرگونه is an adverbial phrase 'in every way/any kind', and از بیش و کم is a prepositional phrase meaning 'about more and less' (i.e. 'about greater and lesser matters' or 'great and small matters'). The whole clause describes the scope and variety of the conversation.
segments.s650-g191: به کسری سپردند یکسر سخن is arranged with the indirect object first: به کسری 'to Khosrow', سپردند is past 3rd-person plural 'they entrusted/handed over', یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/wholly', and سخن here means 'the matter/discussion' (abstract noun). Literally: 'They entrusted the whole matter to Khosrow.'
segments.s650-g192: خردمند و دانندگان کهن is a nominal phrase describing the agents: خردمند 'wise (ones)' and دانندگان 'those who know/experts' joined by و, with کهن 'ancient/old' qualifying دانندگان (or the group as a whole). In context this names the assembled wise, ancient scholars who performed the action (they are the implied subject of سپردند).
segments.s650-g145: چنان دان is an imperative phrase: چنان (so/thus) + دان (know). که introduces the following content clause. In کسری نه بر دین ماست the negation نه precedes the prepositional phrase; بر + noun + است forms a stative clause indicating 'has authority over / is over' (بر here = 'over/against'). ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s650-g146: ز دین سر کشیدن is a verbal-nominal phrase meaning 'to withdraw/turn away from the religion' (ز = 'from'). سر کشیدن here is an idiomatic compound ('سر' + کشیدن) expressing turning away; کشیدن is used with a motion/withdrawal sense. ورا is a clitic meaning 'to/for him'. کی سزاست is a rhetorical question 'what/what sort of punishment is deserved' with سزاست = 'it is deserved/fitting'; کی has an interrogative force here.
segments.s650-g147: یکی here functions as 'someone / one' (an indefinite agent). خط دستش is a genitive phrase 'a line/handwriting of his / a written pledge from his hand' (خط = handwriting/line; دست = hand; ـش = his). بباید is the modal 'must/ought' and ستد is a transitive verbal form meaning 'to take/exact' (here 'to exact/take a pledge').
segments.s650-g148: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'). سر بازگرداند is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to turn back/return' where سر (literally 'head') is used with بازگرداند (to make/let return) — here meaning 'he may turn back'. از راه بد is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the bad/evil way'. بازگرداند is a subjunctive/optative sense of the verb after که expressing purpose.
segments.s650-g149: بپیچاند is a third-person singular present/jussive form (بـ- prefix + پیچاند) meaning 'may/must turn aside' or 'turn away'. از راستی 'from righteousness' is the source phrase (از = 'from'; راستی = 'truth/righteousness'). پنج چیز is the object 'five things'. The verb is causative/active: 'turn five things away from truth.'
segments.s650-g150: که introduces an explanatory clause. دانا is the subject 'the wise (one)'. بر این پنج نفزود نیز contains نفزود, a negative past form (ن‑ + فزود) meaning 'did not add/increase'; بر here means 'to/toward' (i.e. 'add to'), این = 'these', and نیز = 'also/too'. The clause says the wise did not add anything beyond these five.
segments.s650-g151: This is a nominal/existential listing introduced by کجا 'where'. The speaker lists vices: رشک (envy), کین (hatred/vengeance), خشم (anger) and نیاز (need/longing/desire). است functions as the copula 'there is/are' in the listing.
segments.s650-g152: به پنجم 'to/with the fifth' (the fifth vice) sets up the final element; که گردَد is a subordinate verb meaning 'that becomes' or 'if it comes to be' (گردد = become/happen). بر او چیره means 'dominant over him' (چیره = prevailing/dominant) and آز names the fifth vice: greed. The construction expresses a conditional/result: when the fifth (greed) becomes dominant over him.
segments.s650-g209: گوا کرد is a past, literary verb phrase meaning 'made (someone) witness / appointed as witnesses' (گوا = witness; کرد = did/made). The direct object of کرد is the coordinated noun phrase زرمهر و خرداد followed by the object marker را, so the whole segment reads 'he made Zarmahr and Khordad (as witnesses).'
segments.s650-g210: This continues the object list (three coordinated items) finished with the object marker را, i.e. '…and Frāyīn and Banduy and Behzad (as well)'. The conjunction و links the names and را at the end scopes over the whole coordinated phrase.
segments.s650-g211: وز آن جایگه شد به ایوان خویش is a temporal/spatial phrase plus verb: وز (archaic/poetic 'from') + آن جایگه ('that place/position') + شد (past of شدن, here 'went/was moved') + به ایوان خویش ('to his iwan/hall'). The preposition به introduces the destination and خویش (خوی + ش) marks possession 'his own'.
segments.s650-g212: نگه داشت is a compound verbal idea meaning 'kept/held' (نگه = keeping/watch; داشت = had/kept), followed by آن راست پیمان خویش 'that right/true covenant of his' (آن = that; راست = true/right; پیمان = covenant/pledge; خویش marked by ش = his own). Grammatically, the verb precedes the object phrase in this order common in Persian literary style.
segments.s650-g213: به شبگیر is a temporal phrase 'at nightfall/nocturnally' (به + شبگیر). چون is a simile marker 'as/like', and شید is an adjective/noun 'love‑mad, rapt, or beloved'; بنمود (literary past of بنمودن) is 'showed/displayed', with تاج (crown) as its direct object. Word order is temporal phrase → simile → verb → object.
segments.s650-g214: زمین شد is 'the earth became' (subject + past of شدن). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / like', where کردار is used in the sense 'manner/aspect', and دریای عاج 'a sea of ivory' is the simile complement. The clause means 'the earth was transformed as if an ivory sea.'
segments.s650-g215: همی‌راند is an archaic/epic past progressive form (همی‑ prefix + راند) meaning 'was riding/driving on'. فرزند شاه جهان is a genitival noun phrase 'the son of the king of the world' (فرزند = son/descendant; شاه جهان = king of the world), serving as the subject of the verb.
segments.s650-g216: سخن‌گوی is a noun 'spokesman/speaker'; با introduces the accompanying group 'with', followed by موبدان (mōbeds, Zoroastrian priests) and و ردان (and the rādān). Grammatically this is a noun phrase indicating accompaniment: 'the spokesman with the mōbeds and the rādān.'
segments.s650-g1: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' placed before the subject (poetic/inverted order). یکی مرد مزدک به نام is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain man, Mazdak by name' where به نام ('by name') marks the following name as an appositive.
segments.s650-g2: سخنگوی is an epithet/appellative 'the Speaker' linked to the name in the previous segment. با introduces a list of accompanying traits ('with'), and the nouns دانش، رای، کام are coordinated qualities (knowledge, counsel/opinion, and desire/will) that modify or describe him.
segments.s650-g3: This nominal phrase presents two appositive descriptions: گرانمایه مردی 'a man of great worth' (adjective + noun) and دانش‌فروش 'seller of knowledge' (a compound meaning someone who trades or peddles learning). Grammatically these are descriptive predicates introducing the same person.
segments.s650-g4: قباد دلاور is the subject (Qobad the valiant). بدو ('to him') is the indirect object and داد is the past verb 'gave'. گوش here is used idiomatically with دادن as 'to give ear' = 'to listen/attend', so the clause means 'Qobad the valiant gave him his ear' (listened to him). Note the object follows the verb, a normal order in Persian poetry.
segments.s650-g5: به نزد جهاندار gives direction/purpose 'to the sovereign/in the presence of the sovereign'. دستور گشت is a semi-passive idiom: گشت (became/was made) with دستور (an order) expresses 'an order was issued' or 'he was ordered/brought' — the verb is past 'became/was made'.
segments.s650-g6: نگهبان ... گشت is a clear predicative phrase: 'he became the guardian/keeper' (گشت = 'became'). آن marks the specific treasure(s) ('that treasure') and the coordinated گنج و گنجور lists both 'treasure' and 'treasury' as the things he came to guard.
segments.s650-g7: ز here is causal 'because of', so ز خشکی خورش تنگ شد literally 'because of dryness the food became tight/scarce'. خورش (cooked food/dish) is the affected noun and تنگ شد is the past tense predicate 'became narrow/ scarce' used figuratively for shortage; در جهان 'in the world' gives the scope.
segments.s650-g8: The phrase repeats میان before two nouns for poetic emphasis: میان = 'between/among'. کهان and مهان are the two groups being contrasted or included; each میان + noun frames the scarcity as affecting both circles. The repetition is a rhetorical device common in epic verse.
segments.s650-g241: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). بشنید is the simple past third-person singular 'he heard'. گفتار موبد قباد is a noun phrase where گفتار ('speech') is followed by its apposition/possessor موبد قباد ('the mobed Qobad'), i.e. 'the speech of the mobed Qobad.' Word order here is classical: temporal particle + past verb + object phrase.
segments.s650-g242: برآشفت is an intransitive past verb 'he became angry.' و coordinates to the following clause. اندر سخن is an adverbial phrase 'in speech / in words.' داد داد is idiomatic/reduplicative: داد used as the verb 'gave' with a forceful repetition to mean 'uttered/gave out (a cry or protest)' — a poetic way to mark that he cried out or spoke loudly.
segments.s650-g243: گرانمایه کسری is a noun phrase 'the esteemed/precious Khosrow' (adjective + name). ورا is a clitic/particle meaning 'to him' indicating the person who receives the friendship; یار گشت is a predicate 'became a companion/ally.' The verb گشت is the past 'became.'
segments.s650-g244: دل مرد is 'the man's heart' (noun + possessor in juxtaposition typical of classical Persian). بی‌دین is an attributive adjective 'impious' modifying مرد. پرآزار گشت is the predicate 'became full of trouble/distress' (پرآزار as adjective/adverbial and گشت 'became').
segments.s650-g245: پرآواز گشت is 'became loud-voiced/noisy' (پرآواز = noisy, گشت = became). انجمن is the subject 'the assembly/meeting.' سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial 'one and all / all together' that reinforces that the whole assembly reacted.
segments.s650-g246: که introduces the content/words of the assembly's cry ('that...'). مزدک is the name 'Mazdak.' مبادا is the negative optative 'may it not be / lest.' بر تاجور is a short prepositional phrase ('on/for the merchant'); the verb 'to be/side with' is omitted and understood, so the full sense is 'May Mazdak not be (on the side of) the merchant.'
segments.s650-g247: همی‌دارد is a stative/presentive verb 'he holds / he maintains' (the prefix همی signals ongoing or customary action in classical diction). او is the subject 'he.' دین یزدان تباه is the object clause 'the religion of God [is] corrupt' (دین یزدان = 'religion of God', تباه = 'corrupt/ruined'), with the adjective following the noun in poetic style.
segments.s650-g248: مباد is the negative optative 'may it not be.' اندر این نامور بارگاه is a locative phrase 'in this renowned/celebrated court'; the clause is elliptical (مباد + locative) and means 'may it not be so in this famous court,' with the verb omitted but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s650-g41: چنین گفت is an initial formula meaning 'thus said' (گفت is the simple past 3rd-person verb 'said'). کای is an archaic/vocative particle 'O' used to address a king; it introduces the direct addressee. شاه پیروزبخت is the vocative noun phrase 'king (who is) fortunate' where پیروزبخت is an epithet modifying شاه.
segments.s650-g42: A string of epithets joined by و describing the king: سخنگوی 'one who speaks / eloquent', بیدار 'awake / vigilant', and زیبای تخت 'beautiful / glorious on the throne' (تخت = throne). Word order is noun/adjective clusters following the vocative; the adjectives are conjoined to form a series of descriptive epithets.
segments.s650-g43: سخن گفتم is 'I spoke (a word/speech)' where سخن is the object and گفتم is 1st-person past 'I said/spoke'. The conjunction و links the next clause. پاسخش = 'his answer' (پاسخ + possessive suffix ش). دادی‌ام here is 2nd-person past (دادی) plus the enclitic ام = 'you gave me' (i.e. 'you gave [it] to me').
segments.s650-g44: به is the preposition 'to/for'; پاسخ is 'answer'; در بسته is a noun phrase 'closed door' where در functions as 'door/gate' and بسته 'closed'. بگشادی‌ام is again 2nd-person past (گشادی) with enclitic ام = 'you opened for me' or 'you opened (it) for me.' The whole clause means 'you opened for me the closed door of answer.'
segments.s650-g45: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون is an archaic/poetic item (literally from a root 'to come/appear') used adverbially here and functions like 'now/at this time' in context (its exact nuance is poetic). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that', دستور is 'command/order', باشد is the subjunctive/present-form 'be' (used here to state 'if there is'), and کنون is 'now' reinforcing the temporal sense.
segments.s650-g46: بگوید is a 3rd-person optative/subjunctive or jussive form meaning 'let him say' / 'let him speak' (a permissive/requestive sense). سخن is the object 'speech/words'. پیش تو means 'in front of you / before you / in your presence' (پیش marking the audience). رهنمون 'guide' is the agent expected to speak (either nominative subject or vocative apposition), so the clause reads 'let the guide speak his speech before you.'
segments.s650-g47: بدو is 'to him' or 'unto him' (a dative/indirect object used with imperatives: 'say to him'). گفت is the past/quotative 'said' introducing reported speech. برگو (imperative) is 'speak / tell' (an imperative form commanding speech). و joins the next imperative. لب را مبند is a negative imperative: لب 'lip' + را (object marker) + مبند 'do not close' (negative 2nd-person imperative), so 'do not shut your lips.'
segments.s650-g48: که introduces a reason/explanation 'for/because'. گفتار 'speech/words' is the subject; باشد is subjunctive/present 'may be / will be'; م + را in the word list corresponds to the clitic construction مرا 'to/for me' (object 'me' + object marker), and سودمند is the predicate 'useful / beneficial.' The clause thus gives the rationale: 'for the speech will be beneficial to me.'
segments.s650-g193: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' placed before the verb; بشنید is a 3rd-person singular past form 'he heard'. The sequence is conjunction + verb + subject (بشنید کسری) followed by the directional phrase به نزد قباد meaning 'to the presence of Qobad' (نزد = 'in the presence of').
segments.s650-g194: بیامد is a past 3rd‑person 'he came'. ز here is the poetic preposition meaning 'concerning/about' (not a causal 'because of'); سخن کرد is a light-verb construction where سخن (speech) takes کرد 'did/said' = 'spoke'. یاد (literally 'remembrance') is used idiomatically with سخن کرد to mean 'he mentioned/recalled'.
segments.s650-g195: که is the subordinating 'that/for'; اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. فراز آمد is a poetic collocation (literally 'has come on high') meaning 'has come/has arisen'—the verb آمد governs the clause; آن روزگار is the noun phrase 'that time' (object/subject in inverted poetic order).
segments.s650-g196: که again introduces the content clause. دین بهی را is the definite object ('the good religion') marked by را. کنم is first-person present/subjunctive of کردن used here as an auxiliary-like verb ('I will do/make'), and خواستار is an adjective 'desirous' — together this idiom means 'I will be desirous of making/establishing' (i.e. 'I will seek to establish').
segments.s650-g197: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون is an older/poetic verb/adverb ('should come to pass' / 'appear'); که introduces the subordinate clause. او را بود راستی literally 'truth was for him' — بود is past 'was' and راستی is the noun 'truth' meaning 'truthfulness' or 'that the truth lies with him'.
segments.s650-g198: شود is a present/future-form 'will become' (modal/resultative). دین زردشت is the subject ('the religion of Zoroaster'), and بر کاستی (بر + کاستی) is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into lessness' or 'to diminution'—together 'will be reduced/diminished'.
segments.s650-g199: پذیرم is first-person present 'I accept' (the -م ending marks 1st singular), and من is inserted for emphasis. آن پاک دین is the definite object 'that pure religion'. ورا is the pronominal/directional particle 'it' (to/for it) placed after the object as an enclitic, a common older/poetic order.
