segments.s650-g200: به جان is an idiomatic adverbial 'by my life' / 'with my soul' (expressing solemn commitment). برگزینم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I will choose/select'; the following گزین is a terse imperative or emphatic repetition 'choose!' addressed either to the speaker himself as emphasis or to others, and ورا again is the pronominal 'it'. The pair (برگزینم گزین) is a poetic duplication: a declarative 'I will choose' followed by an imperative/emphatic 'choose it'.
segments.s650-g337: چنین is an adverb meaning "thus/so" placed before the verb; گفت is the reporting verb (past) here; پرسنده را uses را as the definite/direct-object (or indirect-object) marker marking the addressee 'the questioner'; سروبن (a cypress-like person/name) appears after را and functions as the speaker (subject) in post-verbal position — classical Persian often allows subject after the verb.
segments.s650-g338: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'who'); شادان is an adjective 'joyful' describing the speaker's state; بدم here is archaic/poetic and the lexicon entry given doesn't match the expected 'I was' reading, so its precise lexical sense is uncertain in the list (treated as an archaic first-person copula/past); تا is the temporal 'until'; نبودم is past negative 'I was not' (i.e. 'I became/grew'); کهن is an adjective 'old/ancient' modifying the subject—overall: 'that I was joyful until I became old.'
segments.s650-g339: چنین 'thus' modifies the whole clause; سست 'weak' is a predicative adjective; گشتم is the past of گشتن 'I became'; ز is the poetic form of 'from/because of'; نیروی is the ezāfe-like construct 'the force/strength of'; شست here most likely means 'thumb' (literal body-part) producing 'I became weak from the strength/power of the thumb.'
segments.s650-g340: به + پرهیز is an adverbial phrase 'by/through abstinence' or 'in restraint'; و is the coordinator 'and'; با او 'with him' is a prepositional phrase adding accompaniment; مساو is an adjective/noun 'equal' used predicatively; ایچ is an emphatic/negative poetical word meaning 'nothing/none'; دست 'hand' can mean literal hand or 'power/ability' — together the clause says roughly 'by abstinence and with him no hand is equal at all.'
segments.s650-g341: This is a possession statement: دم + noun + دارد = 'has a ...'; دم here is used metonymically for the tail/edge ('tip/edge' in the provided options) of the dragon (اژدها); دارد is the present/past auxiliary 'has/possesses'; و links the second object phrase چنگ شیر 'the claw of a lion' — 'he has a dragon's tail and a lion's claw.'
segments.s650-g342: بخاید is a poetic/archaic verb here meaning roughly 'causes to gape/astonish' (the provided senses list suggests astonishment/gaping); کسی را with را marks the affected person 'someone' as object; که introduces the relative clause 'whom he brings under (control)'; آرد is the present/past stem 'brings'; به + زیر means 'under' or 'under the power of.' So: 'he makes anyone gape whom he brings under him.'
segments.s650-g343: هم‌آواز is a compound meaning 'in unison' or 'harmonious with'; رعدست is a fused form meaning 'is like thunder' (رعد + است); و 'and' links the clauses; هم 'also' emphasizes the following phrase; زور 'strength/might' is the noun; کرگ here is dialectal/poetic (given senses include 'down/nap' or 'bird') and is used poetically to indicate a powerful birdish force — syntactically: 'He is thunder-like and also the might of a (powerful) bird.'
segments.s650-g344: به یک دست is a common Persian idiom 'with one hand' (i.e. by one action); رنج 'suffering' is the object of that instrument phrase; و coordinates the second instrument phrase به یک دست مرگ 'with one hand, death' — together: 'with one hand (he brings) suffering, and with one hand (he brings) death.' The repeated به یک دست frames two parallel results.
segments.s650-g81: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase ب (به, “to/for”) + دین (here intended as این “this”) and marks the indirect object ‘to this servant.’ بنده is the noun ‘servant’ used in a humble/self-referential way. پاسخ چنین داد is the verb phrase: پاسخ (answer) + چنین (thus) + داد (gave), where داد is the simple past ‘gave’ and شاه (king) appears after the verb for poetic word order (V S/O is common in classical Persian).
segments.s650-g82: که introduces the content/relative clause (‘that’ / ‘who’). تریاک‌دارست is an archaic/clitic form of ‘تریاک‌دار است’ = ‘is opium‑holding/uses opium’ (دارَست here = دارَ + است). مرد گناه is a compact nominal phrase meaning ‘a sinful man’ or ‘man of sin’ (مرده/مردِ گناه, the noun + noun-as-adjective relationship), so the clause reads ‘that an opium‑holding man (is) sinful.’
segments.s650-g83: اگر starts a conditional clause ‘if.’ خون این مرد تریاک‌دار is a noun phrase ‘the blood of this opium‑holding man’ (این modifies مرد, and تریاک‌دار is a postpositive adjective ‘opium‑possessing’). Word order is noun + modifiers in classical Persian.
segments.s650-g84: بریزد is the present/subjunctive form of ریختن with the prefix ب- (here ‘be shed / should fall’). The main clause is elliptical: کسی نیست با او شمار — literally ‘no one exists to count/hold reckoning with him.’ کسی نیست means ‘there is no one,’ and با او شمار is an idiomatic nominal phrase ‘to reckon with him / to hold him to account’ where the verb such as کردن is omitted.
segments.s650-g85: چو شد is a temporal clause-head ‘when he became’ (چو = ‘when’), followed by the predicate گرسنه ‘hungry.’ نان بود is ‘bread was [there]’ (بود = past of ‘to be’) and پای زهر is a prepositional/locative phrase literally ‘at the foot/base of poison’ (پای = ‘foot/base of’, زهر = ‘poison’), a poetic way to say bread was what mattered even with poison nearby.
segments.s650-g86: به سیری = ‘for satiety’ (به = ‘for/to,’ سیری = ‘satiation’). نخواهد is the negative present/future ‘he will not want’ (here ‘he does not desire’), ز تریاک = ‘from/opium’ (ز = ‘from/because of’), and بهر = ‘for/the sake of.’ Put together: ‘he will not desire opium for satiety,’ i.e. opium will not satisfy his hunger.
segments.s650-g87: اگر دادگر باشی is a protasis ‘if you are just’; باشی is the present/subjunctive 2nd‑person form of بودن used in conditions. ای شهریار is a vocative address ‘O king,’ placed after the condition for poetic emphasis.
segments.s650-g88: به انبار گندم is a locative phrase ‘in/into the storehouse of wheat’ (به here ‘into/towards’ + انبار گندم). به کار نیاید is an idiom meaning ‘will not be useful / will not come to use’ (نیاید = ‘does not come/become,’ کار = ‘use’), so the clause says the stored wheat will not be needed/used under that just rule.
segments.s650-g169: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so.” گفت is the past-tense verb “said” and is followed by the speaker's name مزدک (Mazdak); this verb–subject order (گفت مزدک) is common in classical Persian narrative. که is the subordinating conjunction introducing reported content; این راه راست (‘this true path’) is the noun phrase that will serve as the subject of the reported clause.
segments.s650-g170: نهانی is an adjective (or adjectival predicate) meaning “hidden/secret”; نداند is a classical 3rd-person negative form of دانستن (here ‘does not know’/‘knows not’). The second clause begins with the coordinating negative نه (reinforced negation) and the prepositional phrase بر دین ماست (‘is on/part of our religion’): بر functions here roughly as “belonging to/on” and ست (in ماست) is the copula “is.” Together the clause says the path ‘knows no secrecy and is not part of our faith.’
segments.s650-g171: همانگه is a temporal adverb meaning “at that moment.” ز is the poetic form of از (‘from/of’) and introduces کسری (Khosrow) as the person being addressed or asked of. بپرسید is the past-tense narrative form “asked” (3rd person) and شاه (the king) is the subject placed after the verb — again typical narrative word order in Ferdowsi.
segments.s650-g172: که introduces the content of the king’s question. از دین ‘from the religion/faith’ + به بگذری uses the verb گذشتن (بگذری) with به to mean “to pass away from / to abandon”; بگذری here expresses the action of leaving the faith. نیست راه is an inverted order of راه نیست (‘there is no way’), so the whole clause asks whether there exists any way to leave the religion.
segments.s650-g173: بدو is a prepositional phrase meaning “to him.” گفت is the narrative past “said” (again listed as the verb), and کسری is the speaker (Khosrow). چو is the temporal conjunction “when,” and یابم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form of یافتن/یابیدن (“I find”); زمان is the noun “time.” The phrase چو یابم زمان = “when I find the time.”
segments.s650-g174: بگویم is the 1st-person subjunctive/future-like form of گفتن used here as “I will say/tell.” که introduces the content clause. کژ است = “is wrong/crooked” (predicative adjective + copula است). یکسر is an adverb “altogether/entirely” modifying گمان (“suspicion/conjecture”), so the clause states that the suspicion is completely mistaken.
segments.s650-g175: چو is “when.” پیدا شود is a passive/impersonal verbal construction (پیدا + شود) meaning “become apparent / be revealed.” کژی و کاستی are the nouns “crookedness/wrongness and deficiency/shortcoming,” joined by و ‘and.’ The clause sets a temporal condition: when these faults appear.
segments.s650-g176: درفشان شود is a poetic expression formed from درفشان (literally ‘banner‑waver’/‘one who displays a banner’ or adjectival ‘shining’) plus شود ‘will be made / will become,’ i.e., ‘will be displayed/unfurled.’ پیش تو means “before you / in your presence.” راستی (“truth” or “uprightness”) is the thing being displayed; the clause therefore means that truth will be revealed or presented before you. The Poem uses inverted order (verb earlier) for effect.
segments.s650-g281: همی‌بود is an archaic/imperfective past marker meaning ‘remained/was’ (continuous past). با شرم is a prepositional phrase ‘with shame’ (شرم = shame/modesty) and چندی is an adverbial ‘for a while’; the subject قباد appears at the end (poetic inversion), so the clause reads ‘Qobad was for a while with shame’ → ‘Qobad was ashamed for a while.’
segments.s650-g282: ز نفرین مزدک is an oblique/genitive phrase ‘of/from Mazdak's curse’ (ز used poetically for از). یاد همی‌کرد is the verb+object construction ‘to make memory’ (یاد کردن = to remember); همی‌کرد is the imperfective past ‘kept/was doing’, so together ‘he kept remembering Mazdak’s curse.’
segments.s650-g283: به درویش marks the indirect object ‘to/for the dervish’; بخشید is the simple past ‘bestowed/gave’; بسیار چیز is the direct object ‘many things’ (poetic word order places the object after the verb here).
segments.s650-g284: بر آتشکده is a locative phrase ‘upon the fire‑temple’; خلعت is the robe of honour (the object) and افگند is the past tense ‘threw/cast’ (subject omitted, understood as the same actor). نیز ‘also’ links this action to the previous gift—‘he also cast a robe on the fire‑temple.’
segments.s650-g285: ز کسری is a causal/ablative phrase ‘because of Khosrow’ (ز used poetically for از/به‌سبب). چنان is a degree adverb ‘so/so greatly’; شاد شد is past ‘became happy/was delighted’; شهریار (appearing after the verb) is the subject ‘the king’ (poetic inversion yielding ‘the king became so glad because of Khosrow’).
segments.s650-g286: که introduces a result clause ‘that’; شاخش is a possessive noun ‘his bough/branch’ (شاخ = bough/branch here), همی is the archaic habitual/continuous particle marking repeated or ongoing action, and گوهر آورد بار is literal ‘brought/produced jewels as a load’ (آورد = brought; بار = load/burden). Together: ‘that even his bough/branch was bringing/bringing forth jewels.’
segments.s650-g287: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial ‘from that time on/thereafter’. همه رای = ‘all counsel/all opinions’; با او is ‘with him’. The final زدی is an archaic poetic past-form of زدن used idiomatically here to mean that the counsel ‘fell to’ or ‘rested with’ him (i.e. he became the holder/arbiter of counsel); the clause therefore means ‘from then on all counsel lay with him.’
segments.s650-g288: سخن هرچه = ‘whatever words/utterances’; گفتی here is a noun-ish/poetic form meaning ‘that was said’ (literally ‘speech/conversation’). از او بشندی is an archaic construction meaning ‘were from him/derived from him’ (بشندی is an older/poetic verbal form; the result reading is ‘whatever was said came from him’).
segments.s650-g129: چنین گفت is a prefatory formula (literally “thus said”); کز = که + ز, a poetic fused form meaning “from/among” or “that from,” introducing source or subset; دین پرستان ما = “our religion‑worshippers/our religious adherents.” Word order is somewhat topicalized: the phrase “from Mazdak/… ” or here “from our worshippers” precedes the rest.
segments.s650-g130: همان is a demonstrative intensifier “that very / the same”; پاکدل is an adjective meaning “pure‑hearted” placed before the noun; زیردستان ما = “our subordinates/subjects” with ما as the possessor following the noun. Together they mark a subset emphasized by همان.
segments.s650-g131: فراوان ز گیتی = “many/from the world” where ز (poetic) marks origin; سران = “chiefs/heads.” بر + درند here gives a vivid image: بر = toward/on/at and درند (a present plural verb) = “they rend/they tear” (literally they tear at or press upon). The clause paints numerous worldly chiefs pressing at the gate.
segments.s650-g132: فرود آوری‌گر is conditional/optative in tone: فرود (descent) + آوری‌گر (agentive/conditional-forming element) ≈ “if/when you bring them down.” ز در بگذرند = “they pass through/from the door,” with در meaning “door/gate” and بگذرند (subjunctive/imperative‑like) meaning “they pass.” The clause states the consequence or action if they are brought down.
segments.s650-g133: The clause Z مزدک شنید این سخن‌ها قباد is slightly inverted for narrative effect: ز مزدک (= “from Mazdak”) is the source, شنید = “heard,” این سخن‌ها = “these words,” and قباد (Qobad) is the subject placed at the end. Literal order: “From Mazdak heard these words Qobad.”
segments.s650-g134: به سالار فرمود = “he said/commanded to the commander” where به marks the indirect object; فرمود here functions as a past tense verb “he ordered/said.” تا introduces a purpose/result clause “so that/in order that,” followed by بار داد “gave the load” (داد = gave). The whole means he instructed the commander to hand over the burden/load.
segments.s650-g135: چنین گفت مزدک = “thus spoke Mazdak,” a standard narrative formula. به marks the indirect object (به پرمایه شاه = “to the wealthy/ample‑resourced king”). Note گفت appears before the subject مزدک (inversion) in this poetic register.
segments.s650-g136: که introduces the content clause: این جای تنگست = “this place is narrow/cramped” where تنگست is the archaic/poetic copular form (تنگ + ـست). و چندان سپاه = “and so many troops,” with چندان functioning as a quantifier/adverb (“so many/so great”) modifying سپاه (army/host); the copula or verb is elided in the second conjunct.
segments.s650-g217: به آیین is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with ceremony' (به here as a manner marker). به ایوان شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the king's iwan' (به = 'toward/to', ایوان 'iwan/porch', شاه 'the king'). آمدند is the 3rd person plural simple past 'they came/arrived' and appears clause-final, as typical in Persian.
segments.s650-g218: سخن‌گوی و جوینده راه is a coordination: 'the spokesman and the seeker of the way' (سخن‌گو = 'spokesman/speaker'; جوینده راه = 'seeker of the road/way', راه is the noun modified by جوینده). آمدند repeats the 3rd person plural past verb 'they came/arrived'; verb-final order is preserved.
segments.s650-g219: دلارای is an epithet 'beloved/charming' placed before the name (appositive). مزدک is the proper name. سوی is a directional preposition 'toward/to', and کیقباد is the addressee/name of the king; the whole phrase means 'Beloved Mazdak came to/toward Kay Qobad.'
segments.s650-g220: بیامد is the simple past 'he came' (here idiomatically 'he came forward' or 'appeared' and introduces his action). سخن را uses the definite/object marker را with سخن 'speech' (so 'his speech' / 'the speech' as object/topic). در اندرگشاد is a locative phrase 'in the spacious/wide-open (hall)'. The clause means he came and addressed/delivered his speech in the spacious hall; poetic order places the object before the verb.
segments.s650-g221: چنین گفت is 'thus said/spoke' with چنین ('thus') preceding the verb گفت (past of گفتن) — an inversion where the verb precedes the subject. کسری is the subject/name (Khosrow). به پیش گروه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of the assembly' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', گروه = 'group/assembly').
segments.s650-g222: به مزدک marks the addressee 'to Mazdak' (به = 'to'). که‌ای is an archaic/question form meaning 'who are you?' (که = interrogative 'who', ای a vocative/old form), followed by مرد دانش‌پژوه 'O man of learning' (مرد = 'man/person', دانش‌پژوه = 'scholar/learner'). Together it is a direct address/question: 'Who are you, man of learning?'
segments.s650-g223: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite noun). دین نو is 'a new religion' (دین 'religion' + نو 'new'). ساختی is 2nd person singular past 'you made/you fashioned' (address to Mazdak). پر زیان is an adjectival phrase 'full of harm' (پر = 'full (of)', زیان = 'harm/detriment') that qualifies the religion.
segments.s650-g224: نهادی here is a 2nd person past 'you placed/established' (you set into place). زن و خواسته is a coordinated object 'woman and desire' (زن = 'woman', خواسته = 'desire/wish'). در میان is a locative phrase 'in the midst' or 'in the middle', indicating where they were placed. The clause accuses the addressee of putting woman and desire at the center of that new religion.
segments.s650-g321: بر آن انجمن is a prepositional phrase meaning 'at/in that assembly' (بر = on/upon + آن = that + انجمن = assembly). نامه is the direct object placed before the verb, a normal Persian order in older/poetic style. برخواندند is a past plural verb with the prefix بر- producing the sense 'read aloud' here (an archaic/poetic compound), so the whole clause means 'they read the letter aloud at that assembly.'
segments.s650-g322: ولیعهد را uses را to mark the definite direct object 'the crown prince.' شاد is an adjective used adverbially here ('joyful' / 'merrily') modifying the following verb. بنشاندند is past plural, causative/putting-into-seat 'they seated/installed.' Word order is object + adverbial adjective + verb, common in Persian narrative.
segments.s650-g323: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' کسری is the proper name (Khosrow) and functions as subject. نشست is the simple past 'he sat/took his seat.' از بر گاه نو is a conventional poetic locution: از + بر + گاه (گاه here part of the compound 'برگاه' = seat/throne) + نو = 'upon the new throne'; the sequence 'از بر' is an idiomatic poetic framing meaning 'on/upon.'
segments.s650-g324: همی‌خواندندی is a progressive/imperfective past plural with the classical prefix همی- meaning 'they were singing/chanting.' ورا is the indirect-object 'to him.' شاه نو ('the new king') is what they were chanting/calling him; in Persian the complement follows the indirect-object. The verb form and prefix are archaic/poetic.
segments.s650-g325: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'for/to his kingship' (به = to/for; شاهی = kingship). بر او 'upon him.' آفرین خواندند is an idiom: آفرین (acclamation) + خواندند (they uttered/called out); here خواندند is used in the sense 'to call/cry out' with آفرین as the shouted phrase. The verb is past plural.
segments.s650-g326: به سر برش is the phrase 'upon his head' (به سر = onto the head; برش here functions as 'بر + ش (enclitic = his)' in classical/poetic usage). Note: the provided lemma senses for برش do not match this enclitic usage, so an index of -1 is used below. گوهر is the object 'gems/jewels.' برافشاندند is past plural 'they showered/sprinkled,' with the prefix بَرا- indicating an action of scattering over.
segments.s650-g327: ورا نام کردند is the common Persian causative expression 'they gave him a name / they named him' (ورا = to him, نام = name, کردند = they made/did). نوشین روان is a compound personal name: نوشین 'sweet' modifies روان; روان here is used in the sense 'flowing/spirited' as part of the name.
segments.s650-g328: که introduces an explanatory/cause clause here ('for/because'). مهتر جوان بود = 'he was the chief/foremost of the young' (مهتر = chief/head; جوان used as the noun 'young men'). بود is the past-tense copula 'was.' و دولت جوان continues in parallel: 'and (his) fortune was young/ youthful' — دولت (fortune/position) + جوان (young) with the copula elided by parallelism.
segments.s650-g9: ز (a poetic preposition) + رویِ هوا is a locative image: literally 'from the face/surface of the air' meaning 'from the sky'. شد is the past of شدن used as a copular/change-of-state verb 'became' (here functionally 'vanished'), and ناپدید is a predicative adjective placed after the verb: 'the cloud became vanished' = 'the clouds vanished.'
segments.s650-g10: به ایران is a prepositional/locative phrase ('to/into Iran' used here as the location 'in Iran'), کسی + negated past verb ندید yields 'no one saw' (کسی takes the negative to mean 'no one'); برف و باران is the compound object 'snow and rain.' ندید is the past negative form 'did not see.'
segments.s650-g11: مهان جهان is a genitive/attributive phrase ('the guests of the world') with juxtaposition instead of an ezāfe (poetic style). بر در is a locative phrase 'at/on the door/gate' and Ki Qobad (کیقباد) names whose gate is meant: 'at Kay Qobad's gate.'
segments.s650-g12: همی is an archaic/adverbial marker indicating continued or habitual action ('was/kept on'); هر کسی is the distributive subject 'each person'; آب و نان is the thing remembered and precedes the light-verb phrase کرد یاد, where کرد (to do/make) + یاد (memory) form a light-verb construction meaning 'remembered' or 'kept in mind.' Word order follows Persian norm: object/phrase before the verb.
segments.s650-g13: بدیشان ('to them') is a prepositional/pronominal compound marking the indirect object; چنین گفت is the formula 'thus said'; مزدک is the speaker. که introduces the reported clause ('that...') and connects the quotation/subordinate content.
segments.s650-g14: نماید is a third-person singular form used optatively/obliquely here after که ('that') to express what the king will/do 'may do' (classical subjunctive/optative sense). شما را: شما is the object 'you' and را is the definite object marker. به امید راه is a prepositional phrase 'in/with the hope of a way/path'—altogether 'that the king will make for you a way of hope' (i.e. grant you a hopeful path).
segments.s650-g15: دوان is an adverb/verb form 'running/rushed', اندر آمد is 'came in/entered' (an older locative verb phrase), and بر شهریار indicates direction/toward the king ('into the king's presence' or 'to the king'). The whole segment describes a sudden entrance toward the ruler.
segments.s650-g16: چنین گفت is the narrative formula 'thus he spoke/said'; کای is the vocative particle 'O' (classical address), followed by the epithet نامور شهریار 'renowned king' used directly after the vocative to address the sovereign.
segments.s650-g153: تو ('you') begins a conditional clause. چون here functions as 'if/when' introducing that condition. چیره is an adjectival/predicative stem meaning 'dominant/overmastering', and باشی is the second-person singular present-subjunctive/optative form of بودن used in subordinate conditional clauses ('you be / you are'). بر governs the following object with the sense 'over/against'. این پنج دیو is the noun phrase 'these five demons' (object of بر).
segments.s650-g154: پدید آیدت is a verb phrase 'will appear/manifest to you': پدید (manifest/appear) + آید (third-person present) with the enclitic -ت 'to you' attached to the verb. راه is the noun 'path/way' and stands after the verb (verb-first order is common in classical Persian poetic lines). کیهان خدیو is an epithet modifying راه—'the ruler/sovereign of the cosmos/world'—so the whole means 'a path to the ruler of the world will appear to you.'
segments.s650-g155: از این پنج literally 'of these five' (از marks source/partitive). ما را is an experiential/dative construction meaning 'to us' or 'upon us' (Persian often uses 'X را ... است' to mean 'X has befallen us' or 'we have X'). زن و خواسته is the compound subject 'woman and desire', and است is the copula 'is/has (there)'. The whole clause expresses that, among those five, woman and desire have come upon/afflicted us.
segments.s650-g156: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause 'that'. دین بهی is a genitival phrase 'the good/right religion' (بهی = 'good' or 'true'). در جهان is the locative 'in the world', and کاسته است is a perfect/resultative expression meaning 'has been diminished' or 'has decreased' (کاسته, from کاستن, with the copula است). Together: 'that the good religion in the world has been diminished.'
segments.s650-g157: زن و خواسته ('woman and desire') is a coordinated noun phrase. باشد is the present-subjunctive/optative third-person singular of بودن used here to express a hortative/assumptive sense ('let there be' / 'may it be' / 'be'). اندر میان is a locative phrase meaning 'in the middle' or 'among (you)'. The line thus states that woman and desire may be present in the middle/among people.
segments.s650-g158: چو here means 'provided that / if'. دین بهی را uses را as the object marker on 'the good religion'. نخواهی is the second-person singular negative/future form of خواستن here meaning 'you will not want / you do not wish', and زیان ('harm, detriment') is the object of that desire. The clause therefore means 'if you do not wish harm to the good religion' (a proviso attached to the previous statement).
segments.s650-g159: کز is a compound/archaic preposition meaning 'from' or 'from which' introducing the causal source. این دو 'these two' names the two previously mentioned items (woman and desire). بود is the past/copular verb 'was' or 'there was/there came', and the following nouns رشک و آز و نیاز ('envy, greed and need/longing') are the result or products. The structure says that envy, greed and longing arose from those two.
segments.s650-g160: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which'. با + خشم و کین is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with anger and hatred'. اندر آید is a present/habitual verbal phrase 'comes in / enters', and به راز is an adverbial phrase meaning 'secretly' or 'in secret'. So the clause qualifies the preceding nouns: these (affections) come secretly together with anger and hatred.
segments.s650-g137: همانا is an emphatic adverb meaning 'verily/indeed' at the start of the clause. نگنجند is a 3rd‑person plural negative present/past‑narrative form of گنجیدن ('to fit, to belong') meaning 'they do not fit / are not suitable'; در پیش شاه literally 'in the king's presence' (در + پیش used here as a locative phrase). Together the segment says 'indeed they do not belong before the king.'
segments.s650-g138: This clause contains two coordinated actions with the implied subject 'he' (the king): به هامون خرامد = 'he strode to/into the plain' (به + place + خرامد, a past/epic verb 'he strode proudly'); کندشان نگاه is a light‑verb construction where کند ('he makes/does') takes نگاه ('a look/gaze') as its object and the clitic شان 'them' as the object pronoun — 'he casts his gaze on them.' Word order is poetic but the meaning is 'he strode onto the plain and looked at them.'
segments.s650-g139: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he said/commanded' used here as 'he ordered.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'); تخت بیرون برند is the clause governed by تا, where تخت 'the throne' is the object and برند is the 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/imperative of بردن 'to carry/take' — 'that they carry the throne out.'
segments.s650-g140: ز ایوان شاهی is a source phrase: ز (poetic 'from') + ایوان شاهی 'the royal iwan/porch'; به هامون برند repeats the directional goal with به 'to/into' + هامون 'plain' and the same verb برند 'they carry/take'. The segment completes the order: 'take it from the royal iwan to the plain.'
segments.s650-g141: به دشت آمد is a simple past clause 'came to the plain' (به + place + آمد). از مزدکی صدهزار is the source/agent phrase 'one hundred thousand from Mazdak' (from the followers of Mazdak), with the numeral/collective صدهزار placed after the noun phrase — a common poetic word order.
segments.s650-g142: برفتند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they went/advanced.' شادان is a postpositive adverb/adjective 'joyful, rejoicing' modifying their action. بر شهریار uses بر in the sense 'toward' + شهریار 'the king' to indicate direction ('toward the king').
segments.s650-g143: چنین at the start means 'thus/so.' گفت is the verb 'said' (here used in narrative), followed by the speaker مزدک. به شاه زمین is the indirect object 'to the king of the land' (به + شاه + زمین, with زمین qualifying شاه as 'of the land/realm'). Note the verb precedes the subject in the poetic inversion 'thus said Mazdak to the king.'
segments.s650-g144: This is direct address introduced by که‌ای (here read as که ای = 'O you who...'). برتر از دانش is a comparative phrase 'superior to knowledge' (برتر + از + دانش). بآفرین is an optative/exclamatory form ('may you be praised' / 'bravo') directed at the addressee. Together it reads as a praise/wish: 'May you, who are above knowledge, be praised!'
segments.s650-g257: که here introduces the command clause ('that' / 'so that') leading into an imperative. با این سران places the means/objects of the action ('with these chiefs') with the preposition با + demonstrative این + plural noun سران. هرچه خواهی is a free relative ('whatever you want') where هرچه ('whatever') is followed by خواهی (2nd person singular present/desired action 'you want'). بکن is the imperative of کردن ('do'), completing the command; word order is object/adverbial + relative phrase + imperative, typical for Persian imperative emphasis.
segments.s650-g258: از این پس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'from now on' ('از' = from + 'این پس' = after this). ز before مزدک is the poetic/pre-classical form of از meaning 'concerning/of' or 'about' (here 'of/about Mazdak'). مگردان is a negative imperative (do not turn/do not return) used here idiomatically with سخن ('speech') to mean 'do not speak'. The verb precedes the noun سخن in this poetic order, so the full idea is 'do not speak (speak not) of Mazdak'.
segments.s650-g259: به درگاه کسری is a locative/locution 'at (the) court/presence of Khosrow' (به + درگاه + proper name کسری). یکی باغ بود is an indefinite nominal clause 'there was a certain/a garden' where یکی = 'a certain' precedes the noun باغ and بود is the past copula 'was'. Classical Persian often places the locative phrase before the predicate as here.
segments.s650-g260: که begins a relative clause modifying باغ ('whose/which'), introducing the property of the garden. دیوار او 'its wall' uses او as a possessive pronoun after the noun (classical placement). برتر از is the comparative 'higher/superior to', followed by راغ (here used poetically as 'longing/desire'). بود is the past copula 'was', completing the descriptive relative clause.
segments.s650-g261: همی is a durative adverb meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'. گرد بر گرد او is a repetitive locative phrase 'around about it' (گرد = around; بر گرد + او = around it), emphasizing encirclement. کنده کرد is a verbal compound: کنده (the past participle 'uprooted/dug out') plus کرد (the past auxiliary 'did'), yielding the simple past 'he uprooted/dug up'. The verb phrase is transitive and here the direct object is implied or deferred to the next clause.
segments.s650-g262: مر is an archaic clitic (listed senses 'me' / 'to me') used here as a pronominal/dative marker attached before the noun phrase; in this context it functions adverbially or datively and links the action to the narrator's perspective. این مردمان را is the definite object 'these people' with را marking the direct object. پراگنده کرد is the past tense 'he scattered' (پراگنده = scattered/adjective + کرد auxiliary). Together: '(and) he scattered these people (with respect to me/here)'.
segments.s650-g263: بکشتندشان is the past plural verb 'they killed/slain them' where the object clitic -شان ('them') is attached to the verb. هم here adds emphasis ('also' / 'indeed' / 'just so'), and به سان درخت is a simile 'like/as a tree' (به سان = like; درخت = tree). The clause compares the slaughter to the felling or treatment of a tree.
segments.s650-g264: زبر پای is a two-word descriptive phrase where زبر ('rough' / 'coarse') modifies پای ('base/foot'), i.e. 'rough at the base'. و is the conjunction 'and'. زیرش ('under it' / 'beneath it') uses the possessive suffix -ش = 'its'. سرآگنده is a compound adjective 'head-raised' = 'proud/with head high', and سخت ('hard/firm') further qualifies the image. The sequence lists attributes of the tree as part of the simile for how they were killed.
segments.s650-g249: از آن دین جهاندار بیزار شد is a poetic word order: از + noun phrase marks the object/source of aversion ('from/with that religion'), جهاندار (lit. 'world‑holder') is the subject 'the sovereign', and بیزار شد is a past‑tense stative construction ('became averse/grew disgusted') with شد as the copular/auxiliary.
segments.s650-g250: This clause uses the archaic/poetic preposition ز (short for از) twice. ز کرده = 'from/because of (what he) had done' (کرده = 'that which was done / his deeds'), سرش = 'his head' (سر + enclitic pronoun -ش), پر ز تیمار شد = 'became full of care' (پر = 'full (of)', ز تیمار = 'of care'), with شد again serving as 'became'. The overall meaning is causal: because of his deeds his head was filled with anxiety.
segments.s650-g251: به کسری سپردش همانگاه شاه: سپردش is the past verb سپرد ('entrusted/handed over') with the enclitic object -ش 'him'. به کسری marks the recipient 'to Kisra', همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately', and شاه (the subject) appears after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s650-g252: ابا هرکه او داشت آیین و راه: ابا here functions as a verb 'refuse/reject/forbid' (the subject 'he/ the king' is carried over from the previous clause). هرکه ... داشت is a relative clause 'whoever had/practiced it' (او refers back to 'that [religion]'), آیین و راه = 'ritual and way/method'.
segments.s650-g253: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'); the following relative clause هر کاو بر این دین اوست = 'whoever is of this religion' (هر کاو = 'whoever', بر این دین = 'of/on this religion', اوست = 'is it/it is so'). Note poetic ordering and the demonstrative/3rd‑person pronoun او referring back to the religion.
segments.s650-g254: مبادا introduces a prohibitive/jussive clause 'may it not be / let it not happen'. The phrase یکی را به تن مغز و پوست is elliptical (no overt verb); منظور is 'that anyone (یکی) — with regard to the body (تن), brain (مغز) and skin (پوست)' and the verb is implied (i.e. 'be spared' or 'be left'). را is the definite object marker. The line thus expresses a strong prohibition/wish that no one be merely left with flesh and skin.
segments.s650-g255: بدان is a contracted classical form (ب + آن = 'to/at that'), so بدان راه = 'in that way'. بد نامور is a two‑word adjective phrase 'bad, ill‑famed / notorious'. صدهزار ('one hundred thousand') is a numerical phrase placed here for emphasis and will link to what the king speaks about in the following segment; classical Persian often places such numerals before or after verbs for metrical reasons.
segments.s650-g256: به فرزند گفت آن زمان شهریار: به فرزند = 'to (his) son/sons', گفت is the past 'said', آن زمان = 'at that time', and شهریار (the subject 'the king') is placed at the end by poetic inversion. The verb گفت governs the indirect object به فرزند; the whole segment reports the king speaking at that moment.
segments.s650-g313: تنش را (tan-ash ra) is the direct object: تن "body" + pronominal suffix ش "his", followed by the object marker را. به دیبا is a prepositional phrase expressing the manner/material ('in/with brocade'). بیاراستند is past plural (they placed/they adorned) — an older/poetic form of آراستن; verb comes after its object and its adjuncts in this line.
segments.s650-g314: A simple coordinated list (گل و مشک و کافور) followed by the past-tense imperfective verb می‌خواستند (they desired/wished). The conjunction و strings the items and the verb at the end expresses what they wished for; می‌خواستند here is a past plural form meaning 'they wanted' rather than a future.
segments.s650-g315: یکی here is an indefinite/numeral 'one/a certain'. دخمه کردند is the verb phrase 'they made/constructed a tomb' (دخمه + کردند). شاهنشهی is a postpositive adjective/epithet 'imperial/Kayanian' qualifying the tomb; poetic word order places the epithet after the verb phrase.
segments.s650-g316: Another enumerative line introduced by یکی ('one' or 'a certain [thing]'): تاج شاهی is a noun + attribute ('crown of kingship'), followed by و and تخت مهی ('a throne described by مهی', a poetic adjective meaning 'moon‑like/beautiful'). Adjectives often appear after the noun in the style of the Shahnameh.
segments.s650-g317: نهادند is past plural of نهادن 'they placed'. The prepositional phrase بر تخت زر ('upon the golden throne') gives the location and شاه را (king + object marker) is the patient being placed; Persian order here is verb + locative + object, a poetic inversion but all arguments are present and clear.
segments.s650-g318: ببستند is past plural and here means 'they bound/fastened/secured' (one of its possible senses). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that' (not 'until' here). جاودان is the adjective 'eternal' modifying راه ('the way/course'), and را marks راه as the object of ببستند; the order is inverted for poetic effect (verb — purpose marker — adjective — noun — object marker).
segments.s650-g319: چو here means 'when'. موبد (mōbed) is the Zoroastrian priest; بپردخت (poetic variant of پرداخت) means 'he performed / he paid (the rite)' or 'attended to'. از سوگ شاه literally 'from/of the mourning of the king' functions as a genitive/oblique phrase meaning 'in the mourning for the king' — poetically phrased with از.
segments.s650-g320: نهاد is past of نهادن 'he/she placed/presented' (here 'presented'). آن کیی نامه‌بر is a noun phrase 'that Kayanian letter‑carrier/courier' (کیی = Kayanian, an epithet). پیشگاه is the goal/location 'before the presence/court' — the verb is followed by the actor phrase and then the destination, a common classical order.
segments.s650-g305: ز گفتار او is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on/about his speech' (ز = 'because of/about'); هیچ is an emphatic negative 'at all/never'; مپراگنید is a classical negative present/imperative form (second-person plural) meaning 'do not be anxious/worried'. The whole segment is an imperative prohibition: 'Do not (in the least) be disturbed by what he says.'
segments.s650-g306: بدو is the dative pronoun 'toward him/to him'; شاد باشید is a polite/plural imperative 'be glad' (باشید = imperative/optative); و links the clauses; گنج is the direct object 'treasure' placed before the verb (typical poetic/SOV order); آگنید (imperative plural) = 'make full/imbue (with)' (classical verb آگندن). Together: 'Be glad to him and endow/fill him with treasure.'
segments.s650-g307: بر + noun is the prepositional phrase 'on/upon'; آن نامه‌بر = 'that letter‑carrier / courier'; مهر زرین = 'golden seal' (noun + adjective); نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put'. Poetic SOV order places the object phrase before the verb: 'They placed a golden seal upon that courier.'
segments.s650-g308: بر موبد = 'upon the mobed (priest)'; رام برزین is a proper name (Ram Barzin) following the title; نهاد again is past 'placed'. The clause repeats/continues the action: 'They placed (it) upon the mobed Ram Barzin.' The verb is past and the recipient follows the preposition.
segments.s650-g309: به هشتاد شد سالیان قباد is a poetic word order for 'Qobad's years became eighty.' به + numeral expresses attainment of an age ('to eighty'), شد = 'became', سالیان = 'years', with the subject قباد at the end. This means 'Qobad reached the age of eighty.'
segments.s650-g310: نبد is a negative/existential verb 'there was not' or 'did not have'; روز پیری = 'a day of old age' (i.e., a period of infirm old age); هم = 'even/also'; از introduces the relation to مرگ ('about/with respect to death' or 'because of death'); شاد = 'glad'. The clause means: 'He had no days of feeble old age, and indeed he was glad of death.'
segments.s650-g311: بمرد is a classical narrative form meaning 'he died' (here used as the past action); و = 'and'; جهان مردری ماند = 'the world was left with his manliness' (مردری = 'manliness/valor', ماند = 'remained'). از اوی = 'from him/of him' (indicating the source of that legacy). The clause says that after his death his manliness remained in the world.
segments.s650-g312: شد is a past/inchoative 'there came/it became' used here with inverted order; از چهر و بیناییش = 'from his face and his sight/eyes' (source phrase); رنگ و بوی = 'color and scent' (rhetorical pair). The inversion and context imply that color and scent left his face/eyes (i.e. signs of life departed).
segments.s650-g201: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. راه فریدون is a genitive phrase 'the way of Fereydun' (poetic speech omits the ezâfe). شود (from شدن) is the present/impersonal 'becomes' and takes the adjectival predicate نادرست 'wrong/Corrupt' after it; the clause therefore reads 'when Fereydun's way becomes corrupt.'
segments.s650-g202: This is a nominal/categorial clause ending in the copula است: the subject (عزیر) is followed by its predicate(s) مسیحی و هم زند with هم 'also' marking an additional affiliation; the final است 'is' asserts the state. Poetic style drops the ezâfe and clusters nouns/adjectives before the copula.
segments.s650-g203: سخن گفتن is a nominalized phrase 'the speaking / the speech' (infinitive گفتن used as a noun), with مزدک as its agent (poetically juxtaposed rather than linked by an explicit ezâfe). آید 'comes' here means 'comes to be' or 'takes effect', and به جای means 'in place (of) / instead'.
segments.s650-g204: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / there should not be'. به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world'. جز او 'except him' marks the single exception, and رهنمای is a predicate noun 'guide' placed at the end (typical Persian order: negation/modal + locative + exception + predicate).
segments.s650-g205: ور is the conditional 'if'. ایدون (classical/poetic form of آمدن) 'comes to pass / happens'. که introduces a subordinate clause whose subject is او. کژ گوید combines the adjective کژ 'crooked/wrong' with the verb گوید 'says/speaks' (classical present/subjunctive form) to mean 'speaks wrongly'. همی is an emphatic particle. The whole segment = 'if it happens that he speaks wrongly...'.
segments.s650-g206: ره پاک یزدان is a noun phrase 'the pure way of God' (poetic compression omits ezâfe). نجوید is the negative-present form 'does not seek' (classical formation with ن- prefix), and the final همی again adds emphasis; the implied subject is the same 'he' from the previous clause.
segments.s650-g207: به من ده ورا is an imperative clause: ده is the second-person imperative 'give', به من 'to me' is the indirect object, and ورا (ورا = او را) is the direct object 'him' (poetic clitic order). و آنکه در دین اوست is a coordinated relative clause 'and he who is in his religion' with the copula ست 'is' final.
segments.s650-g208: مبادا is a negative wish/precaution 'may it not be / lest'. یکی را is 'one person' with را marking the (definite/emphatic) object. به تن مغز و پوست uses به to indicate the basis or respect 'by / with respect to the body, brain, and skin' (a coordinated list: مغز و پوست). The phrase expresses a wish that no one be judged or reduced merely to body, brain and skin.
segments.s650-g49: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening: چنین (thus) + گفت (said) introduces direct speech; the subject is omitted (implicit 'he'). کای is a vocative particle 'O' used to address someone directly, followed by two epithets (نامور شهریار = 'renowned king'). Word order places the vocative after the speech-verb, a poetic pattern.
segments.s650-g50: کسی را که ... استوار is a relative clause modifying کسی. را is the definite/object marker attached to کسی; که introduces the relative clause 'who/that'. بندی به بند is a poetic expression meaning 'link by link' or 'from link to link' (literally 'a binding to a link'), and استوار is a predicate adjective ('firm/steadfast') with the copula omitted (standard in Persian). Overall pattern: 'someone (definite) who, link by link, is firm.'
segments.s650-g51: خورش (his food/dish) is the object; بازگیرند is a 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive / jussive form meaning 'they take back' or 'they seize'. زو is poetic contraction of از او 'from him' (ز = از 'from', و here stands in the tokenization for the pronoun), and تا introduces a subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive بمرد 'that he die' ('until he die'). The structure expresses duration/continuation: they will seize his food from him up to the point that he dies.
segments.s650-g52: This clause is SOV: به (to) marks the destination/goal, بیچارگی (misery) is the state, جان و تن (life and body) is the definite object marked by را, and سپرد is a past-tense verb 'he entrusted/handed over/consigned'. Together: 'he consigned life and body to misery.' Note the object (جان و تن را) comes before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s650-g53: مکافات is a noun 'recompense/retribution' followed by a genitive-like noun phrase 'آن کس که نان داشت او' ('that man who had bread'). که introduces the relative clause 'who had bread'; داشت is past tense 'had'. The final او is an emphatic pronoun referring back to the man. The whole phrase means 'the recompense of that man who had bread (was)...'.
segments.s650-g54: مر is an older/poetic form of مرا 'to me'; این بسته را is the definite object 'this bundle/package' (را marks it), خوار بگذاشت is a verbal phrase meaning 'made (it) low/despised' or 'treated (it) as worthless' (بگذاشت past 'he put/made'); the final او is emphatic 'he'. Literal order: 'to-me this bundle-OBJ despised he-made' → 'he made this bundle contemptible in my sight.'
segments.s650-g55: This is a rhetorical question. چه means 'what'; باشد here is a modal/conditional auxiliary ('would be' / 'will'), and بگوید is the 3rd-person singular present-subjunctive of 'to say' used to express potential/conditional speech ('would say'). مرا (tokenized here in two pieces) is the object 'to me' and پادشا means 'king/monarch'. The clause asks 'What would the king say to me?' with the subjunctive/conditional coloring.
segments.s650-g56: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. این مرد is the subject 'this man'; دانا, بد, و پارسا are a string of adjectives attributed to him ('wise', 'bad/evil', and 'pious'). Persian omits the copula, so the adjectives follow the noun directly. The juxtaposition creates a paradoxical or complex characterization (wise yet bad and pious).
segments.s650-g17: The clause opens with the prepositional phrase به گیتی (literally 'to/at the world') setting the topic 'about the world'. سخن پرسم is a compact verb phrase 'I ask/speak a question' (پرسم = I ask, an older/classical first‑person form). از تو marks the addressee 'of you / from you', and یکی is a postposed quantifier 'one' specifying a single question; the overall word order places topical phrase before verb, typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s650-g18: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. ایدون is an archaic/poetic temporal/adverbial element ('now/at this time' here; its literal sense is 'come/appear') that narrows the condition to the present moment. که is the complementizer 'that/which', linking to the following verbal phrase. پاسخ‌دهی is the action 'to answer/reply' (here second person: 'you answer'), and اندکی qualifies it as 'a little/briefly'. The whole means 'if you now give a brief answer.'
segments.s650-g19: This is a reportage frame: the proper name قباد is the speaker, سراینده 'the singer/poet' is the addressee/epithet. گفتش is 'he said to him' (گفت + object clitic), and بگوی is the verb of direct speech in the original order (imperative 'speak' or 'say it'); here the sequence is subject → vocative/role → 'he said to him' → reported imperative.
segments.s650-g20: به من (to me/for me) is the indirect object of the imperative تازه کن 'renew/make fresh' (تازه + کن, imperative 'do/make'). در سخن آبروی is a locative/field phrase 'in speech/words, (my) honor' (آبرو = reputation) specifying where the renewal should take place; the verb imperative takes the indirect object before the locative phrase.
segments.s650-g21: بدو گفت is a compressed sequence 'he said to him' (بدو = to him, گفت = said). آن کس که introduces a relative clause 'the one who/whom', followed by مارش گزید where گزید (here transitive) means 'bit/stung' — poetically phrased as 'whose mar (snake) bit him'. The relative clause describes the afflicted person; classical word order places the relative clause after the demonstrative.
segments.s650-g22: همی is an emphatic/continuative particle 'indeed/just/soon'. از تنش 'from his body' (از + تن + possessive clitic ش). جان بخواهد پرید is a periphrastic prediction: جان 'life' + بخواهد (present/future auxiliary 'will/desires') + پرید (leap/escape) — literally 'his life will wish to leap from his body', i.e. 'his life will soon leave his body.' The combination conveys imminent death.
segments.s650-g23: یکی 'one (person)' begins the clause. دیگری را uses دیگری with the object marker را to topicalize or indicate 'another/one of them' as affected. بود is the copula 'was', and پای زهر 'a foot of poison' = 'a poisoned foot' (زهر qualifying پای). In classical/poetic Persian the genitive/qualifying element follows, so پای زهر means 'the foot touched by poison'.
segments.s650-g24: گزیده is a past participle here ('bitten/struck' from گزیدن) describing the afflicted one (the sense 'bitten' is the intended poetic meaning even if the listed lemma senses differ). نیابد is the negative present/subjunctive 'does not/ cannot find' (نیابد = 'he finds not'), ز تریاک 'from opium' (ز = from), and بهر 'benefit/help' — together: 'the bitten one finds no help from opium.' The verb-final negative form and the prepositional source are typical in older Persian.
